title: Chestnut_Lake_Camp_Write_Up
text:
**Chestnut Lake Camp – Program Write-Up** 

**1. Leadership Team and Recent Growth Headlines** 

• **Leadership context:** Aaron Selkow described arriving to lead Chestnut Lake in 2020 (with his wife Anne taking a lead role beginning in 2021). Judith Friedman joined in 2021 as Engagement Director and serves as a first-line contact for prospective families. 

• **Enrollment growth:** The team described reaching ~425–435 campers in first session and 350+ in second session—highlighting a materially larger second session than the camp historically ran. 

• **Retention shift:** Camper retention was described as rising from ~65–68% historically to “over 90%” (with the goal of becoming a true “forever camp,” not a one-and-done trial). 

**2. Session Options and Camper Pipeline** 

• **Core session lengths:** The camp framed itself as primarily a 3-week and 4-week session camp, with a meaningful full-summer option. 

o Full summer was described as ~7 weeks, with ~30% of campers attending the full summer (often after their 2nd or 3rd year). 

• **Discovery Camp (short intro):** A ~5-day / 4-night “Discovery” option (for rising 2nd and also commonly rising 3rd/4th graders) that is placed at the start of the second session. o Leadership emphasized this is not a growth priority; when possible they try to convert families to a full 3-week experience. 

• **Extension from first to second session:** Historically, families could almost always extend from first to second session; the camp noted this is now sometimes limited due to higher occupancy (waitlists/closed divisions were mentioned for some age groups). 

**3. Camper Experience and Daily Structure** 

• **Traditional, well-rounded camp:** Chestnut positions itself as a classic camp experience with a broad activity mix and progressive skill development. 

• **Balanced schedule:** The daily structure was described as roughly half electives and half more structured division-based programming—so campers both choose and also rotate through the full activity set. 

• **Facilities highlighted:** 

o A heated saltwater pool with nighttime lighting, large screens, and music was mentioned as a signature feature.

o Dining facilities were described as a strength; leadership also noted longer-term facility upgrades (e.g., eventual pool replacement) as part of ongoing campus planning. 

**4. Community Culture and Best-Fit Families** 

• **Geographic diversity as a differentiator:** The camp emphasized that many campers arrive without a pre-built friend group; kids should not expect to be bunked with large clusters from the same school. 

o Leadership framed this as a point of pride—supporting independence and broader social mixing. 

• **Best-fit campers:** Families looking for a place their child can return year after year; the camp is supportive and welcoming, but expects campers to engage and participate (the team emphasized “it’s partly the camp and partly the kid”). 

• **Typical starting ages:** The core 3/4/7-week program was described as starting at rising 3rd grade; given high retention, the most common “new” starters are now rising 3rd–5th graders (older new starters can be a fit but are less common). 

**5. Staffing, Safety, and Operations Notes** 

• **International counselor recruiting:** The camp described in-person hiring and relationship-building with staff in the UK, Australia, New Zealand, and Israel (primarily through Camp America). 

o A data point shared on the call: overseas counselor retention was described as the highest of any camp served by Camp America, with 60%+ returning for a second year. 

• **Medical team continuity:** Leadership highlighted high retention across nurses/doctors and strong familiarity with returning families. 

• **Medication management:** The camp described a dedicated medication nurse role and use of a third-party system for labeling/dosing—paired with strong parent compliance. 

**6. Relationship to Trails End and Program “Feel”** 

• **Adjacent camp context:** Chestnut is located next to Trails End in Wayne County and shares some values and philosophical DNA from the broader camp community. 

• **Not explicitly religious:** The team noted that despite some influence from Jewish camping culture, Chestnut does not run explicit religious programming such as a Friday night service. 

**7. Touring and Admissions Touchpoints**

• **Tour availability:** The camp tours frequently, with more limited but still regular winter touring. Judith (Engagement Director) described being heavily involved in tours and prospective-family communication. 

• **Early decision guidance:** Because second-session space can now be tight in some divisions, the team encouraged earlier registration for families who may want to extend or attend full summer.

title: Les_Elfes_International_Write_Up
text:
**Les Elfes International (Verbier, Switzerland) – Write Up** 

**1. Program snapshot and location** 

• **Year-round international camp in Verbier.** Family-run organization that described itself as a “100% kids camp all year round” (not a school). 

• **Verbier setting.** French-speaking Switzerland; the village grows significantly in winter (the call referenced ~30,000 people in winter). 

• **Campus model.** The program described owning/operating its own chalets and having in-house operations (drivers, housekeeping, chefs). 

**2. Getting there and transfers** 

• **Primary gateway.** Geneva airport, followed by an approximately 2-hour drive to Verbier. 

• **Saturday airport presence.** The program described having staff at Geneva airport each Saturday to welcome arriving students (including unaccompanied minors). 

• **Train option.** Families can send students by train; pickups were described as possible from Lausanne (between Geneva and Verbier). 

**3. Seasonal calendar and stay lengths** 

• **Winter camp.** 

o Runs December through April. 

o Arrivals/departures every Saturday (the call referenced Dec 13 through the end of April). • **Spring camp (May).** 

o Combines a week of skiing in Zermatt with a week of summer activities in Verbier. 

o Call noted that spring groups skew heavily from India (~90%), so the international mix can differ vs. winter/summer. 

• **Summer camp.** 

o Runs June through August; the program highlighted an early start (June 8) and a late end (Aug 31). 

o Minimum stay referenced as 2 weeks. 

**4. Age grouping and community mix** 

• **International mix.** The program emphasized a globally diverse camper community and the social value of a multi-country cohort.

• **Age groups (as described).** 

o Typical grouping referenced as 6–10, 11–14, and 15–17. 

o If enrollment supports, it may split to four groups (6–9, 10–12, 13–14, 15–17). 

• **Group size.** The call referenced a maximum of ~40 students per age group (with additional groups added as needed). 

**5. Language program options** 

• **Languages offered.** French, English, German, Spanish (and Chinese in summer, as mentioned). 

• **Provider.** Language classes described as delivered through Verbier Language School. • **Tracks.** 

o Conversational option (referenced as 6 periods/week in winter; 8 periods/week in summer). o Intensive option (in winter, intensive language can replace morning skiing). 

• **Optionality.** Languages were described as optional; students can opt out of language to do additional sports/activities. 

**6. Winter camp: ski & snowboard focus** 

• **Levels and instruction.** Three ski/snowboard levels; instructors described as qualified. 

• **Supervision.** Students were described as never skiing without staff; slope supervision ratio referenced around ~1:8 (and ~1:7 off-slope). 

• **Mountain lunch.** The program described using a mountain restaurant overlooking the Alps for lunch so students can stay on the slopes. 

• **Weekly wrap.** End-of-week race and certificate were mentioned. 

• **Excursions.** Excursions were described as optional in winter; some students may choose to ski instead. 

**7. Summer camp: activities and workshops** 

• **Activity breadth.** The call referenced ~45 activities (examples: rock climbing, swimming, mountain biking, aqua park, ropes course, sports day, excursions to Lake Geneva, banana boat). 

• **Opening celebration.** A camp-opening day was described with country flags, bubble machine, inflatables, and a Holi festival. 

• **Workshops (one day per session).** 

o Parents choose a workshop; external experts teach a specific skill. 

o Examples mentioned: etiquette, leadership, cooking, robotics, art/theatre, photography, first aid, fitness.

• **Day camper option.** If families are staying in Verbier, students can attend as day campers (even for a single day, as described). 

**8. Food, equipment, and student support** 

• **Food.** The program described accommodating a wide range of dietary needs (examples: kosher, halal, vegan, lactose-free). 

• **Equipment rental.** Ski/snowboard equipment (skis, boards, poles, helmets) described as rentable on campus; the program mentioned replacing ~30% of gear each year. Ski clothing rental was also referenced. 

• **Nearby sports facilities.** A sports center about a 2-minute walk was referenced (examples: swimming pool, bouldering hall). 

• **Health & safety.** On-campus nurse and staff first-aid training were referenced, with local medical access and a hospital about ~30 minutes away. Insurance coverage including helicopter transport was mentioned. 

• **Individualized care.** The program stated experience supporting medical/learning needs (examples mentioned: diabetes, growth hormone, ADHD, students on the spectrum).

title: Camp_Akiba_Write_Up
text:
**Camp Akiba Write-Up** 

**1. Big picture** 

• **Re-launch of a legacy camp:** Camp Akiba is being brought back after being inactive for ~20 years, with a very engaged alumni community (weekly/biweekly “canteen” meetups continued even through COVID). 

• **Traditional “brother/sister” model:** Boys and girls run separately during the day (separate dining rooms, pools, ball fields), with coed moments for special events/evening activities. 

• **Full summer length + accessible price:** 6-week season by design (sweet spot for families with earlier school start dates). All-in tuition quoted at **$8,600** (~40% below many nearby comparable camps). 

• **All-inclusive philosophy:** Off-campus trips, laundry, towel service, and most programming are included; a short list of true add-ons (e.g., private tennis/golf) sits outside tuition. 

**2. Camper profile** 

• **Grades/ages:** Currently targeting roughly rising **3rd–8th grade** (they noted not planning to take older than rising 8th grade while re-establishing the culture). 

• **Early years skew younger:** Leadership expects the first couple of summers to be strongest with ~7–12-year-olds as the camp “plants a garden” and grows. 

• **Everyone is new:** In the re-launch year, every camper is a first-time Akiba camper—helpful for social dynamics and expectations-setting. 

**3. Campus, cabins, and indoor space** 

• **Cabins + comfort upgrades:** Renovated cabins with pine walls, ceiling fans/lighting, covered porches, and individual cubbies with outlets; bathrooms/sinks and private showers in every cabin. 

• **Technology-free:** Camp is device-free and enforces it; outlets are intended for small cabin items (speaker/fan), not screens. 

• **Significant indoor footprint:** ~40,000 sq ft of indoor space for rainy days and programming depth. 

• Theater (~6,000 sq ft) with new sound/video; heated and air-conditioned. • Alumni Hall (~7,000 sq ft) for indoor soccer/hockey/basketball, etc. • Gym/weight room with age-appropriate training and a dedicated specialist. • Air-conditioned dining rooms, rec halls, dance studio (“Hilltop”), and canteen area. 

**4. Daily schedule and structure**

• **6-period day:** 2 swim periods (instructional + free swim), 2 athletic periods, and 2 specialty periods. 

• **Instructional swim:** Uses Red Cross system; campers can progress and, if they “test out,” shift into another activity. 

• **Choice built in:** Two weekly choice/registration days (Tuesday on girls’ side, Thursday on boys’ side) where campers select periods in advance “like college registration.” 

• **Evening program:** Leadership described evening activity as a “7th period,” often bringing sides together for camp-wide programming. 

**5. Activities and highlights** 

• **Athletics:** Basketball (full + half courts), soccer, volleyball (including beach volleyball/Newcomb), lacrosse, tennis, pickleball, golf, and gaga. 

• **Waterfront + aquatics:** Two pools (with new large slides), plus a lake program with inflatables/obstacles (slide, climbing element, trampoline, “blob,” obstacle course). • Swim tests for all campers per Red Cross regulations; non-passers can take lessons and re-test. 

• Life-jacket system (color-coded) and buddy checks; ~4 lifeguards stationed at the lake. • Opportunities for lifeguard certification for older campers. 

• **Adventure:** Zip line and climbing elements; camp sources many certified aquatics/adventure staff through its broader “Pocono Valley” group business. 

• **Arts + performance:** Arts & crafts, ceramics, theater/drama, music/dance (supported by indoor theater and studio spaces). 

• **Programs added from feedback:** Motorized water sports (confirmed jet skis; exploring additional options) and an on-site gymnastics program with core equipment (beams/bars, etc.). 

• **Off-site options:** Horseback riding is offered off campus; most trips are included in tuition. 

**6. Food and Jewish life** 

• **Kosher-style dining:** Buffet-style meals, served to campers; nutritionist on staff; salad bar and pasta bar available daily. 

• **Friday night:** Shabbat-style meal (e.g., challah, matzah ball soup, chicken) and a “friendship service” emphasizing community and positive camp citizenship. 

• **Sunday morning:** Bagels and cream cheese. 

• **Religious practice is flexible:** They are not planning routine blessings at meals initially, but may adjust depending on the camper mix. 

**7. Logistics and considerations**

• **6-week commitment:** They generally do **not** plan to offer shorter 3–4 week options (including for international campers), largely to avoid mid-summer friendship disruptions and to support culture-building. 

• **Cabin staffing:** Cabins range ~8–14 campers with at least 2 counselors per cabin; leadership emphasized enthusiastic, highly engaged staff and specialists at each activity area. 

• **Re-launch year note:** Facilities are actively being renovated; leadership committed that new campers will be placed in renovated cabins.

title: Tranquility_Camp_Write_Up
text:
**Tranquility Camp – Write Up** 

**1. Camp identity and philosophy** 

• **Mission-driven, traditional overnight camp.** A nonprofit camp with philanthropic roots—founded in 1919 by Elias Cone to provide a classic summer camp experience at a more accessible price point. 

• **Long-standing community.** Multi-generational families and alumni connections are a big part of camp culture (the camp referenced its 106th summer). 

• **Setting.** Located in the Catskill Mountains of New York. 

**2. Camper profile, size, and session options** 

• **Co-ed community.** Camp described its typical gender mix around ~55/45 to 60/40 (girls/boys varies by year). 

• **Ages.** The youngest campers are typically around 7 (with some 6-year-olds discussed); the oldest campers referenced are around 15. 

• **Capacity.** Approximately 270 campers at full size. 

• **Session lengths.** 

o Full summer: 7 weeks. 

o Partial options: 4-week and 3-week sessions. 

o Flexible departures for early school start (families may leave at ~5–6 weeks, as needed). • **Tuition (as quoted on the call).** 

o 7-week: ~$7,850. 

o 4-week: ~$4,850. 

o 3-week: ~$3,650. 

**3. Program structure, daily schedule, and activities** 

• **Group-based schedule.** Campers move through activities with their group (less emphasis on choosing a single “major” focus). 

• **Typical day.** 

o 3 activity periods in the morning. 

o Lunch together in the dining hall. 

o 2 activity periods in the afternoon. 

o Daily camp-wide swim. 

o Evening program (often a whole-camp activity). 

• **Activity mix (examples mentioned).**

o Waterfront and swimming; boating and fishing. 

o Sports (tennis, basketball, volleyball, pickleball, gaga, etc.). 

o Arts & crafts and performing arts. 

• **Shows and special programming.** 

o Camp referenced running multiple musicals each summer, with Saturday-night shows and rehearsals during the week. 

o Club nights (examples mentioned: running club and nature club). 

**4. Facilities, cabin life, and meals** 

• **Cabins.** Bunks have indoor bathrooms; the call referenced ~3 shower stalls and ~3 toilet stalls per bunk. 

• **Dining hall community.** Campers sit with their counselors; meals are described as family-style. 

• **Camper responsibilities.** Camp mentioned age-based table-setting/serving roles (older campers help with meal service). 

• **Dietary needs.** Camp described accommodating allergies and dietary restrictions (as needed). 

**5. Staffing, supervision, and medical support** 

• **Staffing model.** Mix of general counselors and specialists; camp referenced using Camp America to recruit international specialty staff (e.g., arts, theater). 

• **Health center.** Camp described having two registered nurses (male & female coverage) and an EMT/paramedic on site. 

• **Local medical access.** 

o Pediatrician ~4–5 miles away. 

o Nearest hospital ~20 minutes. 

o Albany Medical Center ~35 minutes. 

**6. Travel logistics and who it can fit well** 

• **Bus transportation.** Many campers (~80%) travel by bus from the NYC area (Queens/central stops were mentioned). 

• **Flying in.** Albany International Airport is ~30–35 minutes from camp; pickups are coordinated. 

• **International campers.** Camp described welcoming some international campers and coordinating travel support when needed. 

• **Best fit.** Classic Catskills camp with structured days, broad activities, and value-oriented tuition.

title: Towanda Write-Up
text:
**Camp Towanda** 

**Program Snapshot** 

Camp Towanda is a **7-week, down-to-earth** coed sleepaway camp in **Wayne County, PA** with a classic, spirited culture and low-frills vibe. Now led by owners/directors **Mendy & Alana Mendelowitz** (Alana is a Towanda alum; ownership began **March 1**), the program is **highly structured for younger campers** and adds **meaningful choice/electives** as campers age (1–3 elective periods/day for older groups). Community is intentionally small-world—**everyone knows everyone**—with **all-camp meals**, frequent **all-camp evening events**, and traditions that keep social pressure low (partial uniform, no makeup culture, one-piece suits at the waterfront). 

**Camper, Staff & Director Profile** 

Towanda welcomes campers starting **rising 2nd–3rd grade**; first-time starters up to **rising 6th/7th** are common and successful. Current enrollment is **~390s** with a plan to grow thoughtfully back to **~500–550**. The camper base spans the **Northeast, Mid-Atlantic, South Florida (60–80 on a shared flight some summers), Colorado, LA, Chicago, Atlanta**, and more. Staff are a mix of **homegrown alumni** and **returning internationals** (UK/Ireland/Scotland/Australia/NZ; support staff from Mexico/Eastern Europe); **senior staff** are long-tenured. Leadership emphasis: **adult-run activity areas** with college-age assistants; **skill and confidence development** over win-at-all-costs. 

**Living Quarters** 

Cabins ring each campus in a **circle layout** that keeps play/social space central. Ongoing **CapEx upgrades** prioritize **bunk bathrooms** (3 finished in 2025; **7–8 more** planned for 2026) and **comfort features** (e.g., exploring bunk fans). Culture minimizes status pressure: **partial uniform** (daytime Towanda tees; themed nudges at night), **no makeup** norm, and a **one-piece policy** for girls at the waterfront. 

**Waterfront & Aquatics** 

Daily lake life with **general swim** and **instructional swim for younger campers**; flexible **evening lake/pool time** being introduced for older campers (e.g., pool parties). Emphasis is on **skill-building** and fun rather than thrill-rides.

**Daily Timetable & Choice** 

Towanda runs a **five-period day** (formerly six). 

● **Wake**: Reveille typically **8:00–8:15am**; optional **“Master” early blocks** (tennis, fitness, basketball reps) for early-risers before reveille. 

● **Day**: Structured periods for lower camp; **older groups choose electives** (1–3/day) in areas like sports broadcasting, podcasting, etc. 

● **End of Day**: **General swim**, team practices, or electives; then spirited **all-camp evening programs** (casino night, hypnotist, celebrations), with **added free-time options for older campers** (e.g., lake/pool). 

Towanda also runs a beloved **Big/Little** program, powerful **all-camp meals**, and frequent **whole-camp evening events** to keep connections tight. 

**Program Highlights** 

**Athletics:** Adult-led instruction across the board; each 45-min block blends **~20 min skill work + ~20–25 min gameplay**. 

● **Wayne County** inter-camp leagues + **friendlies** (Trails End, Starlight, Tyler Hill). 

● **~80+** inter-camp games/tournaments per season. 

**Arts/Activities:** Arts & crafts, culinary, outdoor adventure, sports 

broadcasting/podcasting, and more—each **overseen by an adult head**. **Recognition & Traditions:** 

● **Camper of the Week** (by bunk; criteria vary—welcoming peers, growth turnarounds, effort). 

● **Flagpole shout-outs** (facing fears, sportsmanship, stepping out of comfort zones). ● **Kindness Jibbitz** (custom Crocs charms awarded via peer/staff nominations). **Jewish Life & Values:** Inclusive **Friday Night service** (reflective, music-led), **Friday Night Flicks** highlight video, and weekly values woven into camp life—welcoming to Jewish and non-Jewish families alike. 

**Health, Safety & Food**

**Medical: MD on site**, **6–8 nurses**, plus **3–4 student nurses** (who also counsel) to expand coverage. **Med tent** outside the infirmary for daily meds (reduces clinic traffic). An **Infirmary Manager** supports parent communication and scheduling. 

**Food:** Big push on **quality & options**—a dedicated **salad-/alternatives chef**, abundant **fresh fruit/veg**, and **protein** on the salad bar. Family-style mains with reliable kid-favorites (yes, **pasta** forever). **Peanut butter** is **currently still served** (camp is evaluating policies but not nut-free at present). 

**Vibe & Culture** 

**Old-school, spirited, kind**. Families choose Towanda for **high-quality instruction** without the “flash,” a place where **school dynamics are left at the gate** and kids can **be themselves**. **Everyone knows everyone**, thanks to all-camp meals, nightly events, and the **Big/Little** program. 

**Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extras** 

**Transportation:** Multiple **bus pickups** (Tarrytown/Westchester/CT; North Jersey—Bergen/Paramus; **Livingston**/Central NJ; **Long Island; Manhattan; Philly/South Jersey; DMV**). Coordinated **airport buses** for out-of-region markets; **South Florida** is a large contingent. 

**Session timing:** A **Wednesday start / Tuesday finish** model eased conflicts for both Florida and NY school calendars. First-summer **4-week option** available; **case-by-case** adjustments (e.g., Georgia school starts, high-school sports) considered—especially in year one. 

**Communication:** New leadership is **more available/approachable** than past norms—**email is best** (Alana & Mendy respond directly). 

**Try-It Experiences: Enhanced Rookie Overnight** (1.5 days) and ongoing **private tours** (booking for **2026/2027** already open). 

**Staffing Note: GGs** (female counselors assigned to the **three youngest boys’ groups**) remain—a nurturing, high-impact tradition. 

**Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary** 

Camp Towanda is perfect for kids who want **classic, spirited summer** with **adult-led instruction**, **tight-knit community**, and **low-pressure culture**. Younger campers benefit from **structure and try-everything exposure**; older campers gain **autonomy** through electives and age-appropriate freedoms. Families who value **character, kindness, and skills over flash** will feel right at home. Towanda’s leadership is refreshing the **comforts (cabins/food)** while **protecting the soul** that alumni love. 

**Internal Insights (Highlights & Lowlights)**

**Highlights:** Authentic, **low-frills culture**; **adult-run** activities; **Big/Little** bonds; strong **Wayne County** slate; **Master** early-morning blocks; thoughtful **electives for older** campers; real **parent access** to directors; active **CapEx** on bathrooms/cabin comfort; **Kindness Jibbitz** + robust recognition; inclusive **Friday night** tradition; **GGs** for youngest boys. 

**Lowlights:** Facilities are **mid-refresh** (103-year-old site—upgrades in phases); **not** nut-free (PB still served); seven weeks can clash with **early-start school districts** (accommodations are case-by-case); waterfront is skills-forward, not thrill-ride-centric.

title: TFT Zoom Write-Up
text:
**Travel For Teens** 

**Program Snapshot** 

Travel For Teens (TFT) runs small-group, educational teen travel programs worldwide, blending safety, fun, and meaningful cultural immersion—“**teaching teens to be travelers, not tourists**.” Founded in **2003** (originally “Travel for Paris”), TFT now offers **~160 programs** across the U.S., 

Europe, Latin America, Asia, and more. Groups are intentionally sized and diverse, accommodations are **central and walkable**, and daily logistics emphasize real-world skills (public transit, ordering from local menus). Phones are permitted for photos/emergencies but discouraged during activities; TFT shares **daily photos and updates** with families. 

**Camper, Staff & Director Profile** 

Typical groups average **14–24 travelers** (cap **24**), with **~75% solo travelers** and a broad geographic/international mix (~**90% U.S., 10% international; 50+ countries** represented this past year). Staff ratio is **1:8** (sometimes **1:7**), and **veteran Trip Directors** are placed on every program; staff **retention ~70%** year-to-year. Leadership is veteran camp- and travel-educated (e.g., Ned Clark), with a culture of structured prep, family communication, and high-touch student support. 

**Living Quarters** 

Hotels/hostels are **clean, safe, and centrally located** to maximize walking and local transit. Rooms are a mix of **doubles/triples/quads**; **every traveler gets their own bed** (exception: specific boat berths on the Croatia program). **Friends traveling together may room together** on request—while maintaining inclusive bunking dynamics for solo travelers. 

**Waterfront & Adventure** 

Program activities vary by destination (e.g., snorkel trips like **Molokini Crater** in Hawaii; sailing in **Croatia**; hiking and outdoor adventure across the U.S., Europe, and Latin America). Adventure elements are paired with cultural exploration and guided learning. 

**Daily Timetable & Choice** 

Days combine **guided cultural immersion**, hands-on learning, and age-appropriate independence under supervision. Groups navigate **public transit**, order from **local menus** (not

fixed tourist menus), and practice travel skills. TFT provides **pre-departure webinars** (packing, expectations, success tips) and on-trip routine/structure so teens know what’s next and how to thrive. 

**Program Highlights** 

● **Signature Destinations & Demand:** Italy (largest volume), Greece, France, Spain, Costa Rica; **Japan** is trending fastest for the coming summer; **Croatia** sold out early last year. 

● **Group Model:** 14–24 travelers; **1:8** staff ratio (sometimes 1:7); **veteran leadership** on every departure. 

● **Culture-First Logistics: Central lodgings**, heavy **walking/public transit**, real dining from **local menus**. 

● **Combinations & Lengths:** Options from **~7–10 days** up to **20–30 days**; travelers can **combine trips** (e.g., two 13-day programs). 

● **Disciplines:** Cultural immersion, language, photography, healthcare, community service, adventure/outdoors. 

● **Parent Visibility: Daily photos + email updates**; responsive Trip Director contact; centralized emergency line. 

**Health, Safety & Food** 

**Safety is #1**: veteran staff leadership, rigorous hiring/training (two **in-person staff trainings**—USA & Europe), clear conduct expectations reinforced with families **pre-trip** (notably reducing behavioral send-homes). Dining emphasizes **authentic local restaurants** with guidance for varied diets; hotels provide reliable **Wi-Fi**. TFT coordinates proactively during unexpected events and communicates with families as needed. 

**Vibe & Culture** 

Purposeful, **non-tour-bus** travel that feels local and confident—**small-group, diverse cohorts**, and supportive staff who manage group dynamics skillfully. Learning is baked in (history, context, language) **with fun first**, so teens absorb more without it feeling “academic.” Strong emphasis on **respectful conduct**, independence-with-supervision, and friendship-building (many alumni return and even room together in college).

**Travel Logistics, Parent Communication, Tech & Extra Costs** 

● **Departures/Arrivals:** For many **international programs**, groups **rendezvous at a gateway (e.g., JFK)** to fly together or meet in-country; **U.S. programs** meet at destination. 

● **Phones:** Allowed (photos/emergency); discouraged during activities/meals. A prior **device-free** pilot did not run due to demand; standard programs remain 

phone-permitted. 

● **Cancellations/Consolidations:** TFT now **offers fewer, fuller departures** to minimize cancellations; may **combine** like itineraries (e.g., Costa Rica) to reach ideal group size. 

● **Air:** Dedicated booking partner available. 

● **Comms: Daily photo links** + trip letters; Trip Director reachable; HQ emergency line monitored by leadership. 

**Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary** 

TFT fits families who want **genuinely immersive travel** with **small-group structure** and **experienced leadership**—not big-bus tourism. Teens learn real travel skills, navigate cities, and **engage with culture** while staying safe and supported. Trip lengths are flexible, from **one week to four+ weeks**, with **smart combinations** to build the perfect itinerary. It’s a strong choice for **first-time solo travelers** and friend groups alike who want **memorable, growth-forward adventures**. 

**Internal Insights (Highlights & Lowlights)** 

**Highlights:** Veteran leadership on every trip; **central, walkable** lodging; public transit mastery; **daily parent photos/updates**; **1:8** ratio with high staff **retention (~70%)**; thoughtful pre-trip family/student prep (behavior markedly improved); **room-with-a-friend allowed**; strong demand in **Italy/Greece/France/Spain/Costa Rica**, rapid growth for **Japan**; flexible **trip combinations** and broader **length options** (including new **~10-day** formats). 

**Lowlights:** Phone-free models lack market traction; occasional trip 

**consolidations/cancellations** (mitigated by tighter slate); some accommodations (e.g., **Croatia boat berths**) have shared-config nuances that require clear expectation-setting.

title: Sports Biz Boot Camp Write-Up
text:
**Sports Biz Boot Camp (Stephen Master)** 

**Program Snapshot** 

An immersive, college-level intro to the sports industry for motivated high-school students (grades 9–12), taught by NYU Stern professor and industry executive Stephen Master. Students learn the business behind teams, leagues, media, sponsorships, analytics, and athlete representation through lectures, Harvard case studies, hands-on projects, and daily guest speakers from across the industry. Formats include 2-day New York City commuter intensives, custom one-day school partnerships, and a 2-week Chicago + New York residential option run with West Coast Connection (WCC) that layers classroom time with high-impact field visits (e.g., Madison Square Garden, Yankee Stadium, Wrigley Field, Big Ten Network). Admission is interest-based with a light application to ensure engaged cohorts. 

**Student, Staff & Instructor Profile** 

● **Students:** Grades 9–12; sports players and non-athletes alike—what matters is curiosity, solid academics, and willingness to participate. 

● **Instructor:** Stephen Master—21 years teaching at NYU Stern (Sports Marketing/Media & Sports Betting), former NBA/NFL/Octagon/Nielsen executive, advisor to University of Michigan’s Sport Management program, and active consultant (e.g., University of Hawaiʻi media strategy; launched Chicago’s Bulls/Blackhawks/White Sox network). 

● **Guest Speakers:** Daily lineup spanning leagues, teams, networks, agencies, fan/merch brands, sports tech, legal, and analytics (examples cited: Fanatics, FanDuel, NBA/NHL/Big Ten, team execs). 

**Daily Timetable & Choice** 

● **NYC 2-Day Boot Camp (commuter):** ~8 hours of content across two days; mix of lecture, case discussions, and breakouts. Bring lunch; snacks/drinks provided (pizza may be added). 

● **Custom School Boot Camps:** 4–6 hours on a school holiday/in-service day; content tailored to the host school. 

● **2-Week Residential (with WCC):** Stephen runs academics **10:00–3:00** (Mon–Fri). WCC handles hotels, meals, evening/weekend programming, transportation, supervision, and

safety. Field trips split into smaller rotating groups for access (e.g., 25 students per visit) and personalized interaction. 

**Curriculum Highlights** 

● **Industry Map:** Paths across teams, leagues, agencies, media, betting, apparel, data/tech, venue ops, college sports, and NIL. 

● **Real-World Case Work:** Harvard case studies (e.g., IMG/athlete management) plus current events (media rights, NIL, conference realignment). 

● **Projects:** Team marketing campaigns, sponsorship pitches, pricing/analytics exercises, and social strategy plans—presented for critique. 

● **Career Design:** Translating skills to roles (e.g., analytics beyond “Moneyball”), building a portfolio, and practicing the informational interview. 

● **Networking as a Skill:** Relationship-first playbook (LinkedIn habits, outreach scripts, follow-up cadence); frequent guest interactions. 

**Site Visits & Speakers (Residential Focus)** 

● **New York:** Madison Square Garden, Yankee Stadium, Fanatics offices (targets). 

● **Chicago:** Wrigley Field, United Center (Bulls/Blackhawks), Big Ten Network, Northwestern (targets). 

Expect senior staff briefings, behind-the-scenes tours, and Q&A tied to daily lessons. 

**Health, Safety & Food** 

● **Residential Operations:** Fully managed by **West Coast Connection** (WCC)—their standard staffing ratios, nursing/logistics, hotels, meals, evening/weekend activities, and intercity transport. 

● **Meals (Day Programs):** Bring lunch; snacks/drinks provided. Dietary needs are easier to manage this way; occasional pizza add-on. 

**Vibe & Culture**

Fast-paced, professional, and highly interactive. Emphasis on curiosity, participation, respectful debate, and practical outcomes. Students leave with contacts, artifacts (slides/decks), and a clearer “why me” career narrative—beyond just “I love sports.” 

**Travel Logistics, Communication, Tech & Costs** 

● **NYC 2-Day Commuter: $850** total (about “$100 per hour of content” per instructor framing). 

● **Custom School Boot Camps: $400–$1,000** depending on hours/scope. 

● **2-Week Residential (Chicago + NYC, July windows):** Targeting **just under $7,000**; includes lodging, meals, activities, field trips, and the **flight between Chicago ↔ NYC**. Families get themselves to Chicago at start and home from NYC at end. Capacity up to ~75 (actual cohorts may be smaller); large university classrooms (NYU in NYC; DePaul/Loyola in Chicago, pending). 

● **Dates:** Two back-to-back two-week options across **July 5–31** (exact session blocks to be finalized). 

**Ideal Fit & Four-Sentence Summary** 

Ideal for ambitious high-schoolers who want a substantive, insider’s look at the sports ecosystem—and who are ready to speak up, collaborate, and be coached. The program blends college-level rigor with real-time industry access and portfolio-worthy projects. The WCC residential adds high-touch field experiences and turnkey logistics. Students finish with clearer career lanes, actionable next steps, and meaningful contacts. 

**Internal Insights (Highlights & Lowlights)** 

**Highlights:** Instructor is a rare dual threat (active operator + veteran professor); daily guest speakers; meaty casework and projects; intentional networking curriculum; marquee field access via WCC; flexible pathways (day, custom, residential). 

**Lowlights:** Residential pricing is premium; big-room lectures can feel large without strong facilitation (mitigated by small-group rotations); day programs are BYO-lunch; admissions expects engaged participation—students seeking a light “fan experience” may find the pace intense.

title: Rein_Teen_Tours_Write_Up
text:
**Rein Teen Tours & Rein Community Service – Write Up** 

**1. Organization snapshot** 

• **Two complementary brands.** Rein Teen Tours (travel tours) and Rein Community Service (service + travel). 

• **Experienced leadership.** The call highlighted a long-tenured team of tour directors and returning staff (examples included directors returning for 10+ summers). 

• **Program philosophy.** Create structured, community-building teen travel experiences that combine culture, adventure, and strong supervision. 

**2. Program categories and age/grade fit (as discussed)** • **Domestic tours.** 

o Eastern Adventure was highlighted as a strong entry point for students finishing 7th–8th grade. 

o 8th graders can be a fit for many domestic tours, with age-appropriate groupings. • **European tours.** 

o Rein referenced having three European tour options. 

o Groups are organized by grade/age (example discussed: 10th graders with 10th graders; many 11th graders together on certain departures). 

• **Community Service programs.** 

o Service trips were discussed in destinations including Costa Rica, Hawaii, and California (Santa Barbara). 

o Service is positioned as meaningful and demanding (the call emphasized this is “not community service light”). 

**3. Late-departure options and demand signals** 

• **Late departures.** Rein described adding multiple programs with later departures to align with families coming out of shorter camp sessions. 

• **Examples mentioned.** 

o Project Costa Rica (14 days) with a late departure date referenced as July 18. o Western Skies Tour (2 weeks) with both early and late departure options. o Greek Getaway (positioned for older teens) with early and late departures. 

• **High-demand programs.** An Italy–Greece itinerary created the prior year was described as selling out within 3–4 weeks; the call suggested adding a second departure due to continued

demand. 

**4. Highlighted tour experiences** 

• **Greek Getaway.** 

o Combines mainland history/culture with island time (as described). 

o A farm-based cooking experience with a local family (picking produce and cooking together) was described as a top-rated activity, followed by Greek dancing/traditions. 

• **Italy–Greece program.** 

o Described as a ~22-day itinerary (Italy + Greece) that filled extremely quickly. • **Costa Rica Adventure Tour (non-service).** 

o Described as an adventure-focused trip (10th year running; historically fills each year). o Costa Rica was described as safe, small, and diverse, with a strong mix of activities. • **European Adventure.** 

o An ~18-day European option was discussed, combining culture/history with adventure. • **Portugal, Greece & the Azores.** 

o A newer itinerary was discussed combining the Greek mainland with Portugal and the Azores Islands. 

**5. Community Service approach and example projects** 

• **Impact-forward service.** Rein emphasized projects that create tangible community benefit and shape teens’ values—often over 2–4 weeks. 

• **Project focus.** The call noted that volunteering with children is the most popular, while certain animal projects are capacity-limited to maintain close supervision ratios. 

• **Project Costa Rica format.** 

o Positioned as different from typical host-family/hostel models. 

o Students stay primarily at Hotel Los Lagos (described as a resort-style property) and go out to service projects in the mornings. 

• **Example projects mentioned.** 

o Wildlife rescue center volunteering. 

o Preschool / children’s projects. 

o Environmental & sustainability work (including building an accessible rainforest path so people with disabilities can experience the jungle). 

• **Program evolution.** Some animal/dog rescue components were described as discontinued due to partner constraints (e.g., property changes or staffing capacity).

**6. Supervision, rooming, and group dynamics** 

• **Rooming strategy (domestic tours).** Rein described pre-assigning rooming for the first stop and intentionally separating friends so that teens meet others right away. 

• **Solo travelers.** The call described placing students coming alone with other same-gender peers who also don’t know each other, to create quick connections. 

• **International tours.** Due to longer initial stays, Rein described allowing friends to room together early in the trip while ensuring solo travelers are placed comfortably. 

**7. What’s included vs. not included** 

• **Airfare.** Round-trip airfare to/from the trip gateway is not included (costs vary by home city). 

• **Intermediate flights.** When a trip includes flights within the itinerary (e.g., some Greece programs), those intermediate flights are included in the program price. 

• **Meals.** Lunches are typically purchased independently on domestic and European tours; lunches are included on community service programs (as stated on the call).

title: Rein Write-Up
text:
**Rein Teen Tours & Rein Community Service – Overview & Key Highlights** 

**Who They Are** 

Rein Teen Tours is a long-established, family-run teen travel provider with **40+ years of experience**. Their reputation centers on: 

● Highly experienced directors and staff (many with 10–20+ years working for Rein) ● Extremely structured itineraries with strong supervision 

● A loyal returning population of teens who often travel with Rein for multiple summers 

The company runs both **teen tours** (domestic + international, adventure-focused) and **community service programs** (Hawaii, Costa Rica, California, hybrid Tour & Service). 

They emphasize **quality, safety, personalized attention, and long-term relationships** with both families and agents. 

**Staffing & Supervision** 

● Full-time year-round team; all have deep familiarity with Rein programs and many were Rein campers themselves. 

● Summer staff includes **school social workers, psychologists, teachers**, and seasoned tour leaders. 

● Tour directors shown in the presentation average **8–15+ years** of experience. 

● Ratios vary by program but remain intentionally low, with small-group leadership and individualized attention. 

● Robust staff training, including team-building consultants Jed & Roz Bach.

**Program Model & Philosophy** 

Rein focuses on: 

● **Balanced itineraries** mixing culture, sightseeing, adventure, and downtime 

● **Age compatibility** (groups are separated by grade/age; 11th graders travel with 11th graders, etc.) 

● **Proactive parent communication** (daily updates, group oversight) 

● **Structured social integration**, including room assignments that prioritize helping solo travelers meet others 

● **High-quality accommodations and meals** 

They also continue to: 

● **Visit all tours during the summer** 

● **Meet all group flights on return** 

● **Book flights for families** so logistics are seamless 

● **Call every family who enrolls** as a welcome and touchpoint 

**New & Noteworthy Programs** 

Rein introduced several new offerings with strong enrollment momentum: **Greece, Portugal & the Azores (18 days)** 

A culture + adventure hybrid: 

● Greek mainland highlights (Delphi, Meteora, Athens, kayaking, cooking classes) ● Lisbon (bike tours, Time Out Market, tuk-tuks, dessert class) 

● Algarve (beaches, surfing, catamaran cruise)

● Azores (canyoneering, hot springs, off-road jeeps) 

Ideal for teens seeking high adventure in an international setting. 

**Greek Getaway (2 weeks)** 

Rapidly filling program featuring: 

● Greek mainland 

● **Santorini** with sunset snorkel catamaran 

● **Naxos** with farm-to-table cooking class and cultural immersion 

Consistently among Rein’s most popular new additions. 

**Southern Spirit (late-July departure)** 

● A 12-day domestic itinerary for younger teens needing an **end-of-summer option**. ● Modeled after the back half of their Eastern Adventure route. 

**Italy & Greece (22 days)** 

Launched last year and sold out in weeks. Now expecting two groups. Classic Italy (Milan, Venice, Florence/Tuscany, Rome) + Greek Islands (Santorini, Naxos, Paros) with a combination of sightseeing, water sports, and iconic cultural sites. 

**European Adventure (1 month)** 

Rein’s signature Europe program, known for: 

● Unique **bookend itinerary: Iceland → Western Europe → Spain** ● Three beach stops (Monaco, Capri/Sorrento, Spain) 

● Balanced approach: cultural highlights + adventure + free time 

● Three full departure groups last year; trending similarly for this summer

Iceland kickoff includes: 

● Blue Lagoon 

● Golden Circle 

● Lava tunnel trekking 

● Whitewater rafting 

Ending in Spain features: 

● Madrid activities (snow tubing in summer is a teen favorite) ● Cultural + adventure days 

**High-Adventure Domestic Programs** 

**Costa Rica Adventure (non-service)** 

A decade-long bestseller. Perfect for thrill-seeking teens: 

● Ziplining, canyoning, rainforest waterslide, surfing, snorkeling ● Whitewater rafting near Manuel Antonio 

● Volcano region exploration 

**Hawaii & Alaska: “Fire and Ice” (32 days)** 

One of Rein’s flagship experiences: 

● Western U.S. → Alaska → Hawaii 

● Glacier cruises, dog sled base camp, sea kayaking 

● Four Hawaiian islands, top-tier resorts, snorkeling, catamaran, water sports

**Hawaiian Caper (21 days)** 

Shorter version of Fire & Ice, without Alaska. 

**Community Service Programs** 

Structured, meaningful service with high-impact projects. Rein emphasizes: ● Authentic, non–“service light” placements 

● Deep cultural engagement 

● Ensuring the experience shapes identity and fosters ongoing service at home 

Families choose from: 

**Project Hawaii** 

● Work with children and local organizations 

● Environmental projects 

● Daily activities + weekend travel across islands 

**Project Costa Rica (2- or 3-week options)** 

● Wildlife rescue center 

● Preschool support 

● Sustainability trail project enabling access for people with disabilities ● Beautiful hotel base; afternoons include rafting, zipline, adventure activities 

**Project California – Santa Barbara (UCSB residence)** ● Live on a college campus

● Work with partner organizations supporting underserved communities ● Surfing, SUP, beach biking, and weekend trips to L.A. 

**Tour & Service Hybrid** 

● Combination of sightseeing, adventure, and multiple service types 

Each service site has its own capacity limits, but Rein has sufficient partners for groups of **10–15 teens per project**, ensuring meaningful involvement even when animal placements are not available. 

**Domestic Teen Tours** 

Still extremely popular and an excellent entry point for younger teens: 

● **Eastern Adventure** for 7th–8th graders 

● **Western Skies** (2-week, also a late departure option) 

● **Mini Tours** (3- or 5-week local East Coast-based programs for rising 7th–8th; only program that accepts rising 6th graders) 

● **Grand Adventure** and other multi-week western routes 

These remain Rein’s “pathway programs” that often retain families for 3–4 summers. 

**Policies, Logistics & Family Support** 

● **Age grouping:** Strict; teens are placed with peers in the same grade. ● **Solo travelers:** Intentionally structured room assignments foster immediate friendships. ● **Medication:** Teens carry their own; staff can hold or remind as needed. No extra cost. ● **What’s not included:**

○ Round-trip airfare (Rein books it; parents pay the airfare price) 

○ Lunches for domestic + European programs (service programs include lunch) ○ Optional airport transfers 

● **Intermediate flights within itineraries ARE included.** 

● **Early Enrollment Pricing** runs through mid-December; fully refundable through January 30. 

**Trends Rein Is Seeing** 

● Teens are increasingly jumping **directly from camp to international programs** rather than starting domestically. 

● Europe, Greece, Portugal/Azores, Hawaii, Fire & Ice, and Costa Rica are the biggest draws. 

● Late-departure options continue to grow in popularity for camp families.

title: Poyntelle Write-Up
text:
**Camp Poyntelle – Overview & Key Highlights** 

**Who They Are** 

Camp Poyntelle is a **traditional, pluralistic Jewish overnight camp** with **77 years of history** and a strong community base in: 

● Long Island (largest single enrollment area) 

● Manhattan 

● Northern & Central New Jersey 

● Westchester 

● Emerging markets: Maryland, Atlanta, Florida, Virginia 

The camp sits on a **private, spectacular 70-acre lake**. Historically, Poyntelle operated on **both sides of the lake**: 

● **Pointelle** side (grades 1–7) 

● **Lewis Village** (grades 8–11) 

For operational reasons, the camp consolidated to one side in 2019. Jeff’s long-term vision is to rebuild enrollment to a point where **both sides of the lake can operate again**, restoring the aspirational “graduate to the far side” experience. 

Poyntelle is known for **joyful, informal Judaism** — strong ruach, Hebrew words integrated casually, Israeli staff presence, and a warm pluralistic environment. Families range from observant day-school homes to secular households where camp is a child’s primary Jewish identity touchpoint. 

**Leadership & Background**

Poyntelle is entering a new era under **Senior Director Jeff Horowitz**, who joined the organization in late fall. 

Jeff brings: 

● 22 years in Jewish camping 

● Full-summer NJY camper and long-time staff member 

● Former NJY teen program director 

● Former Co-Director of Camp Nah-Jee-Wah (K–6, 450 campers) 

● Experience with both younger and older divisions 

● High-energy, articulate, strategic leadership style 

He describes himself as “camp Jewish,” a lifelong camp person, a lover of ruach, and someone eager to modernize programs without losing Poyntelle’s authentic, traditional overnight camp feel. 

**Current Camper Population & Identity** 

Poyntelle serves: 

● A broad range of Jewish identity and observance levels 

● A community that values **inclusive, accessible Jewish camping** 

● Families attracted to **quality programming at a highly competitive price point** 

Philosophy: 

Poyntelle aims to deliver **authentic Jewish camp magic** — joyful, values-driven, communal — without being heavily ritualized or restrictive. 

**Program Philosophy & Vision**

Jeff believes Poyntelle has **excellent bones**, but programming has been **underutilized** and **under-innovated** in recent years. 

His vision includes: 

● Keeping Poyntelle firmly rooted in **traditional camp identity** 

● Dramatically improving **program quality, innovation, and instructional level** ● Offering **more electives and choice**, especially for older campers ● Strengthening Jewish culture (Israeli staff, community moments, values education) ● Enhancing teen leadership, enrichment, and certification opportunities 

Balance: 

Poyntelle will not be a hyper-specialized sports academy or arts academy. Instead: ● High-level instruction (especially with international specialists) 

● Breadth > extreme specialization 

● A camp where kids who like many things can thrive 

**Program Areas & Improvements** 

Poyntelle already has: 

● A beautiful **ropes course** with ziplines and climbing 

● Great **sports fields**, tennis, pickleball, Gaga, baseball, basketball ● A strong **performing arts** and **cooking program** 

● A full **waterfront** with skiing, canoes, kayaks, windsurfing, paddle inflatables ● A **heated pool** 

Jeff is investing in:

● **Expanded lake inflatables** (moving toward an IHC / Westmont-style lake experience) ● More water skis and windsurfing equipment 

● **Outdoor cooking expansion** (pizza oven, fire cooking) 

● **Nature photography & drone-based STEM programming** 

● **Golf offering** (range netting, mini range, or virtual golf TBD for 2026) ● **Sports facility upgrades** (tennis courts already partially redone; more planned) ● **Potential second tier of the ropes course**, including a giant swing ● Cabin refreshes and key infrastructure improvements (drainage, irrigation, site feel) 

All improvements aim to elevate Poyntelle from “traditional but basic” to “traditional but robust, vibrant, and modern.” 

**Jewish Life** 

Poyntelle is: 

● **Pluralistic** 

● **Kosher-style**, with a **fully kosher option** available 

● Not shifting to full kosher in 2026 — Jeff will evaluate after one season ● Focused on **joyful, communal Jewish identity**, not heavy ritual 

Jeff wants stronger: 

● Jewish educational touchpoints 

● Israeli staff presence 

● Cultural programming

● Community values intentionally embedded in camp life 

**Staffing Changes & Direction** 

Returning staff include: 

● Head of Athletics 

● Head of Waterfront 

● Head Counselors for all age groups 

● Assistant Director Madison 

● Many seasonal leaders 

New leadership: 

● **Associate Director Becky Metzger**, New Jersey-based mom of three, strong camp background 

Staffing philosophy shift: 

● Increase **international hires** (stronger skill backgrounds, lower attrition) ● Maintain one or more **general counselors per bunk** (more for younger campers) ● Specialists deliver higher-quality instruction 

● General counselors remain central for supervision, transitions, SEL, and camper care 

**Daily Schedule & Electives** 

A major shift for 2026: 

Younger campers:

● More **instructional swim** (returning after several years) ● More **instructional sports** 

● Reduced mandatory cabin periods 

● One structured activity + **three electives** in the afternoon 

Older campers: 

● Even more elective-heavy 

● Greater flexibility to follow interests 

● More leadership and enrichment options: 

○ Public speaking 

○ Guest speakers 

○ Leadership curriculum 

○ Lifeguard certification (e.g., 8–11am blocks for a week) ○ Service/volunteer opportunities 

Jeff is also rebuilding process pieces: 

● Attendance/check-in systems via **Camtivities** 

● Stronger supervision structure during transition periods 

**Sessions & Enrollment Structure** Current: 

● Strong **full-summer culture** (around **60% of campers**) ● ~30% first session

● ~10% second session 

Goals: 

● Bring second session closer to first’s numbers 

● Maintain healthy full-summer cohort 

● Evaluate adding **2-week intro programs** once Jeff has a full season to assess 

Extensions: 

● Historically, if a family extended, they paid *first session + second session*, not full-summer rate 

● Jeff intends to **change this**, so extending becomes financially incentivized (decision expected shortly) 

**Pricing & Value Proposition** 

Poyntelle is a **non-profit camp**, offering one of the strongest value propositions in the Northeast. 

Approx pricing (Jeff’s notes): 

● Under $10,000 for K–5 

● Around $12,000 for the **Israel + camp** program (3 weeks Israel + 4 weeks camp) 

Positioning: 

● “95% of what you’d expect from a $17–18K private camp at ~60% of the cost.” ● Ideal for families wanting high programming quality without private-camp pricing. 

**Travel & International Community**

Chaperoned flights: 

● **Georgia and Florida** (ongoing, not new for 2026) 

● Flexible to add more as needed for emerging markets 

International campers: 

● Historically: Israel, South America, Europe 

● 2025 saw fewer, but Jeff strongly wants to rebuild these pipelines 

● Camp is well-positioned for families seeking Jewish community in an American summer camp environment 

**Lead Handling & Advisor Process** 

● Jeff will personally handle **all incoming Camp Experts leads** 

● May delegate selectively to Becky depending on family fit 

● Very open communication style (gives out his cell to every family) 

● Understands partnership value and is eager for advisor collaboration ● Enrollment goal is **400–450 campers**; currently around **260–270** 

**Ideal Camper Fit** 

Poyntelle is best for families seeking: 

● A **traditional Jewish overnight camp** with strong ruach 

● Broad programming, not narrow specialization 

● A warm, inclusive community

● Good instruction but not pressure-filled elite sports or arts 

● Reasonable tuition with excellent programming value 

● Plenty of electives, balanced with instruction for skill-building 

● A leadership team focused on elevating quality and culture 

Not ideal for: 

● Families wanting a highly competitive sports academy 

● Kids wanting all-day arts intensives or specialty-only programs 

● Families seeking a luxury or polished private-camp environment 

Poyntelle is **real, warm, joyful, campy, value-driven, and evolving upward** under new leadership.

title: NCOAE_Write_Up
text:
**NCOAE – Teen Leadership Expedition Write-Up** 

**1. Organization, Mission, and Educational Approach** 

• **Who NCOAE is:** 

• NCOAE (National Center for Outdoor & Adventure Education) is based in Wilmington, North Carolina and has operated since 2009. 

• The organization emphasizes outdoor leadership training, environmental ethics, and evidence-based experiential education. 

• **Curriculum and “theory of change”:** 

• Programs are built around a structured curriculum with clear daily objectives, lesson plans, and debriefs (not just “being outdoors”). 

• NCOAE framed its leadership development arc as moving through self, community, action, and impact. 

• **Broader program portfolio:** 

• In addition to teen expeditions, NCOAE runs adult trainings and wilderness medicine offerings (including EMT and wilderness medicine courses). 

• They shared that they serve roughly ~1,000 students per year across programs. 

**2. Teen Expedition Locations and Terrain** 

• **North Carolina (Pisgah National Forest):** 

• Described as a “temperate rainforest” setting with lush terrain and moderate temperatures compared with more arid destinations. 

• **Oregon (high desert + Deschutes River):** 

• Backpacking occurs in high desert terrain; the rafting portion runs on the Deschutes River. 

• Whitewater was described as mostly Class II–III, with features like swim holes and canyon scenery (compared to a “Grand Canyon vibe” without the walls). • **Alaska (remote wilderness):** 

• Alaska programs focus on big, remote landscapes and rugged terrain, with an emphasis on wilderness travel and leadership in a true expedition environment. • **Common thread:** 

• While terrain varies, NCOAE emphasized that core leadership outcomes and educational structure are consistent across locations.

**3. Program Structure and Daily Life in the Field** 

• **Age range and program length:** 

• Primary teen program is a 14-day expedition for ages ~15–17; the team noted that some mature 14-year-olds may be appropriate (often on a case-by-case basis). • They previously ran a shorter program for 13–14-year-olds but have shifted focus to the 14-day model. 

• **Expedition format:** 

• Programs are fully backcountry-based with tent camping throughout; there are no “facilities” in the field. 

• First half: ~5 days backpacking while carrying personal and group gear (food, stove, water purification, etc.). 

• Mid-program: a resupply / transition day where the course director meets the group, swaps out wet/dirty river gear, and reissues clean dry bags, coolers, and boat gear. • Second half: ~5 days rafting/whitewater, living out of boats and camping nightly. • **Pacing and challenge level:** 

• NCOAE stressed they are not trying to maximize daily mileage; time is intentionally allocated to instruction, skill development, and leadership practice. 

• A “bigger” hiking day on the program was described as roughly 3–4 miles, rather than 10–12 miles. 

• **Skill-building and responsibility:** 

• Participants learn practical expedition skills (tent setup, cooking, cleaning, water management) and rotate leadership responsibilities. 

• Evenings include debriefs and guided reflection tied back to the curriculum and leadership objectives. 

**4. Staffing, Safety, and Accreditation** 

• **Staffing model:** 

• Field ratio is approximately 1:6 with two instructors plus a course director involved in the overall program. 

• Instructors are trained in wilderness medicine (Wilderness First Responder and/or Wilderness EMT). 

• **Risk management and water safety:** 

• Risk management was described as the top priority, especially for the rafting portion. • All participants wear helmets and PFDs on the river; staff closely monitor comfort levels and can make adjustments for nervous or non-swimming participants (e.g., a staff “gear boat” traveling alongside). 

• **Accreditations mentioned:** 

• Accredited by the Association for Experiential Education (since 2011).

• Accredited by CAPCE (continuing education accreditation) and in the final stages of an additional school-improvement accreditation process with Cognia. 

**5. Travel, Pick-Up, and Drop-Off Logistics** 

• **Where students meet:** 

• Typical airport meet points mentioned included: Anchorage (Alaska), Portland (Oregon), and Wilmington (North Carolina). 

• They also referenced Raleigh (RDU) as an optional meet point when direct flights make it easier, with a shuttle / bus transfer to the Wilmington campus area (about 2.5 hours). 

• **Arrival and departure windows:** 

• NCOAE coordinates flights into an arrival window and provides group transportation; staff remain on-site until all students have arrived. 

• The same structured approach is used for end-of-program departures. • **Campus-to-field transition:** 

• Participants typically spend time on the Wilmington campus area to check in, sort gear, and align expectations before launching into the field. 

**6. Medical Forms, Medications, and Special Considerations** 

• **Pre-program screening:** 

• Families complete a comprehensive registration package covering medical history, allergies, and medications. 

• The program team reviews these details ahead of time to plan for food, medication management, and any necessary accommodations. 

• **Medication administration:** 

• When appropriate, NCOAE prefers teens to self-administer medications responsibly, but instructors can administer and document medication from program medical kits when needed. 

• Medication administration involves documentation/signature protocols. • **Refrigerated medications:** 

• For medications that require refrigeration (examples mentioned included insulin and growth hormone), NCOAE said it can often be accommodated with planning and coordination with their medical director, typically using specialized coolers/containers.

title: Ivy_Bound_Write_Up
text:
**Ivy Bound Write-Up** 

**1. What Ivy Bound is** 

• **Test prep + tutoring provider:** Ivy Bound focuses on SAT/ACT preparation (and related academic support), pairing students with tutors matched to learning style and goals. 

• **College admissions support:** They also offer college guidance elements like essays, resumes, recommendation strategy, and supplemental-essay planning. 

**2. Approach and tutor matching** 

• **Matching matters:** They intentionally match families with tutors based on preferences (male/female, “militaristic” vs more supportive style, older vs younger tutor) and scheduling needs. 

• **Right-sized prep plans:** A “thorough” SAT plan was described as ~25–30 hours on average; some students may only need ~10 hours if focusing on a single area (e.g., verbal/English). 

**3. Practice tests and review support** 

• **Proctored practice tests:** Ivy Bound runs timed, proctored practice tests that are scored quickly (students leave with a score the day of the test). 

• **Group review sessions:** Follow-up review (e.g., Sunday night after a Saturday test) is available even for students doing private tutoring—an extra layer many standalone tutors don’t provide. 

**4. Guarantee** 

• **Score improvement promise:** For students who have a baseline SAT/PSAT score, Ivy Bound stated they guarantee **50–150** point improvement or **50% refund** (half the money back). 

• **Track record claim:** Presenter noted that in ~20 years, only ~8 times did a “diligent” student miss the target (and those students still improved ~50–60 points). 

**5. Formats they offer** 

• **Private 1:1 tutoring:** Standard option; they noted packages don’t discount heavily because it’s fundamentally tutor time. 

• **Paired tutoring:** Two similarly matched students can work together and split the hourly cost. • **Group classes:** Offered online.

• **“Form your own” classes (custom groups):** If a family can gather a group (e.g., teammates/friends), Ivy Bound can run a custom class in-person (Northeast corridor DC → New Hampshire) or online; hybrid setups are possible (some in-person, others joining remotely); 

• Families select the schedule and the cohort; if there’s enough notice, they can also request a specific tutor. 

• Pricing example shared: a **$1,350** rate for groups of 4+ (with lower pricing for groups of 8+). 

**6. Pricing notes shared on the call** 

• **Enrollment fee:** An “enlistment” fee of **$450** was quoted to be part of Ivy Bound and access their expertise/network (regardless of whether a student does 10 hours or 40 hours). 

• **Hourly ranges:** Tutor rates vary by experience; examples mentioned included roughly **$120/hr** to **$280/hr**, with some tutors at **$300+/hr**. Paired tutoring splits cost between families. 

**7. Additional notes and fit** 

• **Test-optional context:** Ivy Bound’s view: even when tests are optional, strong scores can still be a meaningful “credential” versus peers; they discussed that some highly selective schools have moved back toward requiring tests. 

• **What they don’t do:** They said they generally don’t advise on detailed, niche major/program comparisons across universities (e.g., granular engineering-program rankings), but do handle admissions strategy and application materials. 

• **International:** They can work with international students; TOEFL prep is possible but was described as less common recently.

title: GVI_Write_Up
text:
**GVI (Global Vision International) – Program Write-Up** 

**1. GVI Overview and Program Types** 

• **Organization & mission:** 

• GVI (Global Vision International) runs volunteer-based programs that address global issues through conservation, community development, research, internships, and fellowships, often in partnership with local NGOs. 

• GVI has operated for ~25 years and reported serving 35,000+ participants across its program portfolio. 

• **Under-18 “Teams” programs:** 

• GVI’s under-18 offerings are cohort-based: the standard format is a 14-day program, with one group completing the full program before the next group begins. • Under-18 programs run primarily in the summer (July–August, with some departures in late June depending on destination). 

• Programs are staffed and operated by GVI teams at GVI-managed base locations. • **18+ and gap-year options:** 

• Many of the same destinations also offer year-round 18+ programs (often 1–4 weeks, and in some cases up to ~14 weeks). 

• GVI also runs internships in areas like teaching, community work, and land/marine conservation research. 

• A newer internship option mentioned on the call includes a business-focused track in Tanzania (product development, design, sales, and marketing). 

**2. Under-18 Destinations and Project Themes** 

• **Conservation and research focus (examples discussed):** 

• Canary Islands/Tenerife: boat-based data collection focused on dolphins and whales (marine monitoring). 

• Greece: sea turtle and marine conservation (under-18 runs in a dedicated camp-style set-up near the base). 

• Thailand: combines community development with conservation work that can include sea turtles. 

• South Africa: wildlife conservation with data collection and some local community engagement. 

• **Skills-based add-ons (location dependent):** 

• Some programs include scuba certification (PADI Open Water or Advanced Open Water) as part of a marine-focused itinerary.

• A dive-intensive Mexico program was highlighted as particularly boat- and diving-oriented (not ideal for participants prone to sea-sickness). 

• **New “Journeys” product line:** 

• GVI recently launched a “Journeys” range beginning in Cambodia, with additional destinations planned (India and Cape Town were mentioned as upcoming). • Journeys are designed to combine volunteerism with elements of adventure travel, visiting multiple NGOs over the course of 1–4 weeks. 

• GVI noted the option to create a custom set-departure for Camp Experts families so participants travel together on a dedicated departure. 

**3. Structure, Supervision, and Daily Life** 

• **Cohort structure and group sizes:** 

• Group size varies significantly by destination, based on capacity and accommodation – examples ranged from ~16 to ~40–50 participants. 

• Thailand example: limited availability with groups sometimes split into two groups of 8 for rotating activities. 

• Greece example: peak departures can be large (the call referenced nearly 50 participants across back-to-back departures), managed by multiple coordinators and subgroup rotations. 

• **Staffing model:** 

• Under-18 programs are led by trained GVI coordinators (senior/junior structure was referenced), with additional base staff supporting operations. 

• Under-18 program leaders often also lead university groups and are specifically trained for youth cohorts; they may not be permanently based at a given location year-round. • **Day-to-day rhythm:** 

• Days often start early and include multiple activity blocks (project work, skills, and/or community engagement). 

• Meals are included; on days when activities are far from base, participants may take packed meals. 

• Participants may have light communal responsibilities (e.g., keeping shared spaces tidy) to build shared accountability. 

• In hot climates, schedules can shift (e.g., midday beach time in Greece during peak heat, with project activities later in the afternoon). 

• **Participant boundaries:** 

• Under-18 participants move as a supervised group; GVI emphasized that teens are not permitted to wander town independently. 

**4. Safety, Medical, and Participant Support**

• **Pre-arrival preparation:** 

• After booking, GVI provides a detailed essentials/packing list and pre-arrival support materials (including webinars and short videos) to set expectations by destination. • Parents are included in pre-arrival communications and decision-making support. • **24/7 support and emergency readiness:** 

• Participants and families have access to 24/7 support (including GVI’s partner/support teams and the on-base team). 

• GVI described an incident-prevention approach that tracks “near misses” across bases and runs ongoing drills and mock emergency scenarios to practice response. • **Insurance and medical considerations:** 

• GVI requires participants to carry travel insurance; GVI also described having a comprehensive global insurance policy. 

• Food allergies and other health needs can often be accommodated when disclosed in advance. 

• **Medication handling:** 

• Medication protocols are arranged with families in advance; in some cases participants hold their own medication, and in other cases medication can be stored/dispensed by supervisors depending on parent preference. 

• **Mental health support:** 

• GVI noted employing two full-time psychologists to support mental health needs that may arise during programs (e.g., eating disorders and other challenges), providing professional support beyond the base staff. 

**5. Logistics, Transfers, and Booking Notes** 

• **Program timing:** 

• Under-18 departures are concentrated in summer (July–August, with some late-June starts). 

• Under-18 groups are not mixed; a full 2-week cohort finishes before the next cohort begins. 

• **Airport transfers:** 

• Airport transfers are included for standard arrival/departure dates: participants are transferred as a group from the airport to base at the start and from base back to the airport at the end. 

• Arrivals/departures outside standard program dates can typically be arranged as a custom transfer. 

• **Accommodation:** 

• Accommodation varies by location but is shared for the cohort; under-18 participants do not share accommodation with 18+ participants. 

• Greece was highlighted as a more “camping-style” set-up for under-18 participants, separate from the adult base accommodation.

• **Availability management:** 

• GVI described under-18 programs as high-demand and managed via an occupancy/availability tracking approach. 

• They offered to set up a WhatsApp group for quick availability checks and noted they can sometimes hold a space briefly (e.g., 1–2 days) for a family nearing confirmation. • Additional departure dates may be launched if demand is strong. 

**6. Notable Add-On for Gap Years: Bushwise (South Africa)** 

• **What it is:** 

• Bushwise was described as one of GVI’s fastest-growing divisions, offering a 1-year program in South Africa focused on qualifying as a safari/field guide (with theory + practical field immersion in Big 5 areas). 

• At the time of the call, Bushwise had four campuses with two additional campuses in development. 

• **Placement pathway:** 

• Participants can extend beyond the qualification phase into a practical placement (e.g., an additional ~5 months) at partner lodges across Africa (examples mentioned included Kenya, Congo, and high-end lodges in the Sabi Sands). 

• **Why it’s compelling:** 

• Families value the professional qualification and the depth of field experience; the program can also align well with university pathways related to conservation and field research. 

• A shorter ~28-day version was mentioned as something some universities use as a practical component in degree programs.

title: EXPLO_Write_Up
text:
**EXPLO — Program Write-Up** 

**1. Program Snapshot** 

• **Who it’s for**: Middle school students currently in grades 6–8 (i.e., rising 7th–9th). • **Setting**: Residential (and day) program hosted on the Wellesley College campus (west of Boston), using Wellesley’s dorms, dining hall, and academic spaces. 

• **Program length**: 2-week sessions, with the option to attend back-to-back for a 4-week experience. 

• **Learning style**: Experiential, project-based “workshops” that emphasize curiosity, trying new things, and building confidence in a supportive peer community. 

**2. Academics and Choice Model** 

• **Core structure**: Students typically take two main workshops plus electives (including a weekly elective period) and a wide range of clubs/activities. 

• **Range of topics**: Mix of STEM, humanities, creative and hands-on offerings; the call highlighted “focused” options like Medicine and Veterinary Science, in addition to the broad “360” format. 

• **Student agency**: Electives can be changed if a choice isn’t the right fit; program is designed to feel low-pressure and exploratory. 

**3. Daily Rhythm and Community Life** 

• **Typical flow**: Morning community time in “living groups,” two workshop blocks, lunch, an elective period, then clubs/leagues/afternoon activities; evenings feature community events. • **Residential life**: Students connect with residential advisors on their floors; dorms include common spaces and practical supports like laundry access (and orientation to using it). • **Town access**: When appropriate, students can sign out (with staff oversight) to nearby Wellesley Square for a short outing and to practice independence. 

**4. Trips, Events, and Signature Experiences** 

• **Weekend trips**: Program runs multiple trip options at once (typically 4–5 choices), and uses historic preference data to plan capacity for high-demand trips (e.g., large amusement parks). 

• **Community events**: Evenings are built around shared experiences (examples discussed included bonfires and larger “main event” style programs). 

**5. Support, Supervision, and Technology** 

• **Staffing model**: Dedicated student-life team plus teaching staff; some faculty live on campus as part of the residential community. 

• **Phone policy**: Students call home at arrival and then phones are collected to reduce screen time and encourage face-to-face friendships and community-building. 

• **Arrival logistics**: Families share travel plans in advance; staff track expected arrivals and contact families if a student’s travel timeline deviates from plan.

title: EXSPORTISE_Write_Up
text:
**EXSPORTISE – Write Up** 

**1. Program snapshot** 

• **Residential English + sports academies.** EXSPORTISE runs summer programs at UK boarding schools combining professional sports coaching with optional English language classes. 

• **Origins.** Founded in 1988 by Bram (Dutch) and Steve Bachelor (a Great Britain hockey player/Olympic medalist), with a focus on high-quality coaching and international community. 

• **International mix.** The call referenced 80+ nationalities and overall student satisfaction around 4.5/5. 

**2. UK centres and typical age bands (as described on the call)** • **Clayesmore School (near Bournemouth).** 

o Junior centre; typically ages 9–15. 

o Home of the Multi-Activity option (added recently). 

• **Oundle School (about an hour from Cambridge).** 

o Senior centre; typically ages 11–17. 

o Older students (roughly 15–17) may do supervised town time, depending on behaviour and maturity. 

• **Seaford College (near Brighton).** 

o Typically ages 10–17. 

o Large centre with a wide range of sports—often a good fit for siblings/friends with different interests. 

• **Worth School (near Gatwick / London).** 

o Typically ages 9–16; about ~15 minutes from Gatwick. 

o Convenient for families who want London sightseeing during drop-off/pick-up. 

**3. Course formats: English + sport, or sport-only** • **English Language Plus.** 

o Typically ~15 hours/week of English + ~15 hours/week of a chosen academy (often described as ~3 hours/day each). 

o English placement testing on arrival and end-of-course certification. 

• **Academy-only (no English).** 

o Single sport academies can run at ~30 hours/week (often described as ~6 hours/day).

o Two-sport combinations: ~15 hours/week per sport (often described as ~3 hours/day per sport). 

• **Experience expectations.** Many academies expect some prior experience (the call referenced ~1 year as a rule of thumb; instrumental music similarly expects experience). 

• **Optional external English testing.**
