o Electives include unique options like Lego-building, photography, chess, and  cooking (e.g., baking challah for Shabbat). 

***Waterfront and Sports*** 

• Extensive waterfront program with kayaking, wakeboarding, tubing, sailing, and an  aqua park. 

• Operates four speedboats to accommodate demand for water sports. • Sports leagues introduced, offering a structured rec league for popular activities  like soccer, with age-appropriate groupings. 

***Innovative Additions*** 

• **Cooking Program:** Led by Pascal’s mother, focusing on Jewish and Israeli dishes,  with plans for expansion. 

• **Nature Center:** Includes animals like Dominic the donkey and goats, providing a  connection to nature.

**Staff and Medical Support** 

***Staff Recruitment and Training*** 

• **Counselors:** 

o General counselors often return as staff after a mandatory one-year gap  post-graduation. 

o Specialists (e.g., coaches, lifeguards) are recruited internationally, primarily  from Australia, New Zealand, and the UK. 

• **Hiring Process:** 

o Former campers undergo a rigorous interview process and must complete  training alongside other hires. 

o Emphasis on creating a welcoming and experienced staff culture. ***Medical and Wellness Center*** 

• A state-of-the-art infirmary with a hospital-grade system tracks camper health and  provides proactive care. 

o Alerts for recurring symptoms ensure timely intervention. 

o A team of 4–5 nurses and two daily-visiting nurse practitioners ensures  comprehensive care. 

o Advanced systems handle pre-dispensed medications and enable local  medical professionals to access camper information for efficient treatment. • Nightly camper reports by counselors are reviewed by administrators to address  homesickness, social challenges, or other needs. 

**Safety and Security** 

• **Enhanced Measures:** 

o Sophisticated camera systems, motion detectors, and automatic gates. o Partnerships with local law enforcement and EMS. 

• **Staffing:** 

o Former Marshal of Maine heads security, supported by off-duty police  officers who rotate and reside on camp. 

• Security extends to off-camp trips, with officers accompanying campers.

**Key Focus Areas** 

• **Camper Experience:** The administration prioritizes time spent addressing current  campers' needs over offering frequent tours. 

• **Community Atmosphere:** Activities and transitions are supervised to ensure safety  and age-appropriate engagement. 

• **Facilities:** Regularly updated to support diverse interests, from athletics to arts and  outdoor pursuits.

title: Loop Abroad Recap
text:
**Program Overview** 

• **Core Mission:** To provide pre-veterinary students with hands-on learning  experiences while promoting ethical animal care and global conservation. • **Key Achievements:** 

o Largest provider of pre-veterinary internships globally. 

o Alumni at nearly every U.S. veterinary school. 

o Over $1 million provided to international conservation partners in 2023. • **Focus on Sustainability:** Partners exclusively with locally run and owned  organizations for all international programs to support long-term community  development. 

**Understanding the Veterinary Career Path** 

• **Veterinary School Insights:** 

o Most competitive post-college pathway in the U.S., with only 31 schools and  ~4,000 new students annually. 

o Students must log **“vet hours”** (volunteering, internships, or research)  starting in high school to enhance their applications. 

o Students categorize their hours into **vet hours, animal experience hours,  and research hours**. 

• **High School Students:** Many join Loop Abroad to determine if veterinary medicine  is the right career for them, gaining early exposure to the field. 

**Program Structure and Details** 

***1. High School Programs (Ages 14+)*** 

• **International Programs:** 

o **Thailand:** Two weeks at an elephant sanctuary and a dog clinic. 

▪ Focus: Elephant rounds, dog rescue work, and cultural activities. ▪ Cost: $4,000 (airfare not included); optional college credit ($500). o **Australia:** Two-week wildlife-focused program at a sanctuary. 

▪ Activities: Wildlife husbandry, veterinary techniques, and  

conservation practices.

• **Domestic Programs (WorldStrides Partnership):** 

o One-week programs in the U.S., including: 

▪ Marine Biology (Florida): Focus on coral reef and aquatic veterinary  science. 

▪ Equine Medicine (New York): Comprehensive horse care and medical  practices. 

▪ Zoo Programs (Texas and Massachusetts): Work with exotic animals  in a zoo setting. 

o Staggered schedules allow students to attend multiple programs. o Costs vary by program; all are all-inclusive except airfare. 

***2. Middle School Programs (Grades 6-8)*** 

• **Locations:** Washington, DC, and Texas. 

• Options include both day and residential formats. 

• Focused on introducing younger students to veterinary and animal sciences. ***3. College-Level and Gap Year Programs (18+)*** 

• **Semester-Long Programs:** 12-13 weeks, offering up to 18 credits. • **Summer Travel Program:** Three months across South Africa, Thailand, and Costa  Rica. 

• **Short-Term Programs:** Two-week options in 11 countries, available year-round. • **Specialized Marine Science Programs:** 

o Scuba certification and marine research in Thailand and Costa Rica. o Belize snorkeling-based marine biology program. 

***4. Virtual Asynchronous Programs*** 

• Self-paced courses covering various animal science topics. 

• Designed for high school students (11th/12th grade) and above. 

• Affordable ($1,000 or less) with optional college credit. 

**Program Features** 

• **Teaching Approach:**

o Curriculum includes lectures, hands-on labs, animal husbandry, veterinary  shadowing, and clinical rotations. 

o All programs are led by veterinarians whose primary role is teaching. o High faculty-to-student ratio (maximum 10-15 students per vet). 

• **Ethical Practices:** 

o Focus on animal welfare, with no exploitative activities like elephant riding. o Students learn proper animal handling for medical purposes. 

**Target Audience** 

• Primarily empathetic, science-oriented, and animal-loving students. • Programs cater to individuals who are passionate about animals but may feel  socially disconnected in traditional school settings. 

**Key Program Examples** 

**1. Thailand:** 

a. A week at Elephant Nature Park and a week at a dog rescue clinic. b. Accommodations: Comfortable hotels and basic housing at the sanctuary. **2. Costa Rica:** 

a. A mix of wildlife rescue work and marine science. 

b. Accommodations are more rustic but safe and functional. 

**3. Australia:** 

a. Focused on wildlife rescue and conservation efforts. 

**Inclusion and Support** 

• **Dietary Needs:** 

o Accommodates vegetarian, vegan, kosher, and allergy-related dietary  restrictions. 

• **Neurodivergent Support:** 

o Structured schedules and clear expectations make programs accessible. o Empathetic and inclusive peer groups ensure students feel supported. • **Accessibility:** 

o Accommodates medical needs (e.g., refrigerators for medications, epipens).

**Emergency Protocols** 

• **Tiered Response System:** 

o Site directors handle on-the-ground emergencies, with oversight from global  operations. 

o Medical incidents involve immediate staff accompaniment. 

• **Staffing:** Sufficient staff ensure smooth program operation even during  emergencies. 

• **Insurance:** All students must have medical insurance, including evacuation  coverage. 

**Additional Information** 

• **Flight Details:** 

o Thailand: ~$1,900 for group flights from New York. 

o Costa Rica: $300-$800 depending on U.S. location. 

o Australia: Group flights from Los Angeles. 

• **Accommodations:** 

o U.S. Programs: Primarily college campuses or mid-range hotels. 

o International Programs: Mix of hotels and site-based housing. 

• **Program Scheduling:** 

o High school programs begin at age 14; middle school programs start at 6th  grade. 

o Programs are designed to accommodate different schedules and needs.

title: InvestIN
text:
**Snapshot** 

InvestIN is a London-based summer school specializing in immersive, practical career experiences rather than academic or language-based study. Founded in 2012 by a lawyer and an investment banker who realized their own careers did not match what they expected, InvestIN now offers 15 distinct career-specific summer experiences for students aged 12–18. 

Programs run in central London, delivered by real industry professionals, with hands-on simulations, workplace visits, and professional mentoring. The goal is to help teenagers understand the reality of a career before choosing a university path. 

**Core Approach: How InvestIN Works** 

InvestIN programs are built on three pillars: 

1\. Practical, hands-on career simulations 

Students *do* the job rather than just hear about it. 

Example: Clinical psychology students diagnose actors playing patients. 

2\. Industry-relevant site visits 

Examples include the Emirates Football Stadium for sports psychology students, an airfield for engineering students, Woodlands Farm Trust for veterinary students, and Sky Studio Campus for filmmaking. 

3\. Teaching exclusively by working professionals 

Every session is run by industry practitioners (e.g., doctors, lawyers, engineers, bankers), giving students genuine insight into career pathways, skills, and realities. 

Programs are updated annually based on professional feedback to stay current (including significant AI additions to computing for 2025). 

**Career Programs Offered (Ages 15–18)** 

InvestIN offers 15 career tracks — the broadest portfolio on the market. Key updates include: ● Revamped Computer Science with new AI-driven content

● Merged Art + Fashion into a single Art & Design portfolio program 

● Added TV production elements to Filmmaking 

● New Media Manager Summer Experience covering social media strategy, PR, journalism, podcast creation 

Programs available July–August. Not all run on every date. 

Big Six careers (run on all sessions): 

Doctor, Engineering, Entrepreneur, Investment Banker, Law, Psychology. 

**Format, Schedule & Experience Structure** 

Daily rhythm for residential students: 

● 8:00: Breakfast 

● 10:00–10:30: Escorted walk to venue 

● 11:00–5:00: Career experience (workshops, simulations, visits) 

● 5:30–8:00: Free time around central London + dinner 

● 8:00–10:00: Evening activities (quiz night, movie night, group events) 

Weekend options vary: 

● One-week programs = weekend free time + social activities 

● Two- or three-week premium programs = additional career development content + a gala dinner with professionals from multiple fields 

**Accommodation & Safeguarding** 

Accommodation is at the University of London halls, giving students authentic university-life exposure.

Key features: 

● Private ensuite bedrooms 

● Breakfast and dinner included 

● Nurse on-site 

● 24/7 safeguarding + pastoral PAL (Pastoral & Activity Lead) groups 

● Staff escort students to/from experience venues daily 

● Students may explore local shops/cafés but must return by 8 pm 

**Career Discovery Program (Ages 12–14)** 

A new partnership with Summer Boarding Courses (SBC), based at Dulwich College. 

Ideal for younger teens who want a gentler environment outside central London while still trying multiple careers. 

Structure: 

● Each day introduces a new career field (doctor, engineer, business/finance, computer science, entrepreneur, etc.) 

● Uses InvestIN’s professionals and practical simulations 

● SBC manages accommodation, pastoral care, and the campus experience ● Shared or private rooms available (private at added cost) 

● English requirement: B1 

Confirms Dulwich dates: 

● 6–20 July 

● 20 July–3 August

Becca is confirming whether day students may leave independently (tube home) or must be collected; expected to be flexible with parental consent. 

**Student Outcomes** 

All students receive: 

● A signed reference letter from a professional in their field 

● A Level 3 Qualification in Work Experience & Career Planning (optional), worth 8 UCAS points and recognized globally 

● Industry insight, professional network connections, and real-world experience helpful for university essays and interviews 

**Group Mix & Gender Balance** 

Cohorts are fully mixed across gender and nationality. 

Gender varies by career (e.g., more boys in computer science; more girls in psychology), but social events mix all programs together through structured PAL groups. 

**Dates & Booking Patterns** 

2025 key dates: 

● July 6 (Big Six only) 

● July 27 (all 15 careers available — best date for Camp Experts families) ● August 10 (Big Six only, psychology runs only twice) 

Two-week programs are strongly recommended because content is cumulative; 70–80% of students book two weeks. Accommodation often prioritizes two-week bookings as summer approaches.

**Fees & Discounts** 

Program tiers: 

● One-week summer experience 

● Two-week summer experience (recommended) 

● Three-week premium program 

Discounts: 

● 20% early bird through December 31 

● 25% Black Friday week (largest discount of the year, deposit or full payment required by November 28) 

**Entry Requirements** 

Ages 15–18 summer experiences: 

● English level B2 

● No prior subject knowledge required — only genuine interest 

Career Discovery (12–14): 

● English level B1 

● Interest and curiosity; no experience needed 

**Best-Fit Families** 

InvestIN is ideal for:

● Students exploring potential career paths before choosing IB/A-level subjects or university majors 

● Teens wanting real exposure to medicine, engineering, finance, psychology, law, media, and other competitive fields 

● Families looking for high supervision in London with private ensuite housing 

● Students who prefer structured, professional environments over outdoor or adventure programs 

● 12–14-year-olds needing a softer introduction via Dulwich College before diving into central London programming 

Less ideal for: 

● Students looking for heavy recreation, outdoor adventure, or sports 

● Teens wanting a traditional camp feel; InvestIN feels more like pre-professional training

title: Haileybury Recap
text:
**Haileybury Summer School (UK) — internal program notes (from transcript)** 

**Snapshot** 

● **Provider/Setting:** Haileybury boarding school campus (UK) — summer program positioned as a *“fully immersive taster”* of traditional UK boarding school life, using the **same facilities** as main-school pupils (classrooms, sports centres, common areas). 

● **Established:** Summer school running/growing since **2022** (per speaker). ● **Format:** Mostly **individual enrollments** (they are **not a groups-focused provider**). 

● **Nationality mix:** ~**20–25 nationalities** mentioned; **nationality cap ~15% max** per nationality. Top markets in 2025: **Germany (largest)**, plus **Italy, France, Turkey**, **Vietnamese & Japanese** (some small groups), **Chinese present but not dominant**. 

**Dates & length** 

● **Summer 2026:** Two blocks / up to **4 weeks total** 

○ Runs **5 July – 1 August** 

○ Students can attend **2 weeks or 4 weeks**. 

● **Spring course (new/reinstated for 2026):** 

○ **29 March – 11 April** (1 or 2 weeks) 

○ They were also discussing a possible extra week extension **to 17 April** depending on a Thai group; they preferred students join from **5 April** to create a 2-week cohort. 

**Ages, levels, and course structure** 

**Two summer courses (2026):**

1\. **English Plus** 

○ **Ages: 11–13** 

○ **Minimum English level: A2** 

○ **Style:** Topic- + project-based mornings (subjects taught *through English*), e.g. **science, technology, history, culture, business** (age-appropriate). 

○ **Differentiator mentioned:** Students keep a **daily journal** reviewed by the teacher; if difficulties appear, staff can **adjust class level/content**. 

2\. **Academic English Plus** 

○ **Ages: 14–16** 

○ **Minimum English level: B1** 

○ **Morning “Pathways” (chosen in advance):** 

■ **STEM** (science/tech/engineering/math) OR 

■ **Arts, Business & Humanities** 

○ **Afternoon “Expert Project Class” (chosen in advance):** students pick **one** option per 2-week course (projects build across the full two weeks). 

**Placement / pre-arrival checks** 

● Students submit a **personal statement** + do a **short informal interview** with the **Director of Studies** to confirm level and place them into classes **before arrival**. 

**Academic English Plus — Expert Project options (7)** 

(Chosen **in advance**, **one per course** because it runs over two weeks.) 

● **Global Entrepreneurship** (Shark Tank / Dragon’s Den style): business plan + marketing/finance/HR + P&L; pitch to judges for “investment.”

● **Filmmaking:** group roles (actors/director/script/production/camera/lights); produce a short **2–3 minute film**. 

● **Design & Manufacturing** *(older students only)*: in their Sci/Tech department; build **LED lamp (week 1)** and **Bluetooth speaker (week 2)**; not for younger cohort due to H&S. 

● **International Relations & Debating:** mini **Model UN**-style; big topics (environment, war, AI/tech). 

● **Lead with Language:** English for future academic applications; includes **IELTS tips**. 

● **Science Unlocked:** lab-based projects (examples mentioned: bacteria growth/analysis, DNA work; investigating environmental impacts). 

● **Future Web Creators:** coding + AI + use of robotics/science tech facilities. 

**Activities & evenings (all students)** 

**Weekly “Focus Activities”** (chosen **1 per week**, so **2 per 2-week course**), examples mentioned: 

● **Krav Maga**, **singing & dancing**, **stage/screen acting**, **arts & crafts**, **Bake Off**, **hockey**, **football**, **basketball**, **tennis**. 

They emphasized coaching by **professionals**, and that tennis can be challenging enough for strong players (mini-tournaments). 

**Evenings:** mix of sport-for-fun + socials (examples: **outdoor cinema** with snacks if weather allows; **African drumming**; indoor games on rainy days). 

**Excursions / “Study Tours” (their stated differentiator)** 

● Instead of giving students a worksheet to complete on a trip, they **contract venues** so **museum/venue staff deliver the workshops**. 

● Examples mentioned: 

○ **Fashion & Textile Museum (London)** workshops

○ **Royal Institution of Science** (DNA-related session; students take away a personal “ethnicity” result) 

○ **Cambridge/Oxford** tours led by **Oxbridge graduates** 

○ **Canal Museum** (bridge-building/engineering activity) 

○ **Dickens’ House** (Industrial Revolution context) 

**Accommodation & campus life** 

● Large campus; **separate boys’ and girls’ houses** in different areas of the school. ● Rooms are **mostly single or twin**, with **shared bathrooms**. 

● Some **refurbished houses** in use for summer; **some triple rooms** possible for friends/groups who want to share. 

**Food & dietary needs** 

● “Balanced, healthy” meals; they try to accommodate different nationalities. ● Dietary accommodations mentioned: **gluten-free, kosher, halal**. 

**Pricing (as stated)** 

● **Summer (2 weeks): £4,250** (speaker also referenced “$4,000” earlier, but the clear figure given later was **£4,250**). 

● **Spring (2 weeks): £2,950** (described as “more cost-effective”; fewer museum workshops / more local-London style excursions). 

*(No 4-week summer price was stated in the transcript excerpt, only that the 4-week price was frozen vs 2025.)*

**Transfers (as stated)** 

● Transfers **included** for: 

○ **Heathrow** 

○ **Eurostar** 

● **Gatwick not included** 

● They recommend arriving/departing within a sensible daytime window (avoid very early departures like **6am**). 

**Positioning / who it’s best for** 

● Families wanting a **boarding-school taster** on a prestigious UK campus (not a generic language school vibe). 

● Students who benefit from **structured academics + curated workshops** and a **highly international mix** without large dominating groups. 

● Good fit for: 

○ **11–13** who want topic-based English through subjects + projects 

○ **14–16** who want **STEM or Arts/Business/Humanities pathway** plus a **two-week specialist project**

title: Green Cove Zoom Recap
text:
**CAMP GREEN COVE — TRANSCRIPT RECAP** 

Below is a structured summary of the information shared during the Zoom call about Camp Green  Cove, modeled after the attached template. 

**OVERVIEW AND BACKGROUN**D 

• Ownership & History: Camp Green Cove (girls) and Camp Mondamen (boys) were both founded  by the same family. Mondamen began in 1922; Green Cove followed in 1945. They have  traditionally operated together but split into two businesses for logistical reasons about two years  ago. They still share many resources and cooperate on program elements. Camp Green Cove is  directed by a third-generation family member, and Mondamen is directed by her brother. • Camp Details: Session Lengths: 5-day, 10-day, 3-week, or 5-week sessions (no partial attendance  within a given session). Camper Numbers: 5-day session (~48 campers), 3-week session (~190  campers), 5-week session (~190 campers), 10-day August session (~70–80 campers, with more  availability). Camper Ages: Green Cove accepts girls starting around age 7 or 8 through their teens.  For older first-time campers (15+), they advise families carefully; most older new campers are 13– 14. 

• Geography & Families: Campers primarily come from the Southeast (FL, GA, TX, LA, TN, NC, SC).  Some families come from DC, MD, VA, NY, and a handful from the Midwest, West Coast, and  occasionally international locations. 

**LAKE & WATER ACTIVITIES** 

• Waterfront & Swimming: Located on a lake across from Camp Mondamen. Swimming lessons  follow a “check in / check out” procedure. Features include a zip line, basic canoeing or kayaking  lessons, and a robust safety protocol that includes a “missing camper drill” each session. **INDOOR FACILITIES** 

• Indoor Spaces: Recreation lodge for rainy-day activities such as dance parties, talent shows, or  crafts. Arts & crafts area with plenty of supplies. Dining hall is located in camp; meals are generally  served family-style. 

**PROGRAMMING & DAILY SCHEDULE** 

• Daily Schedule: 7:45am wake-up, then chores and breakfast (~8:30am). Morning assembly with  activity announcements (~9:45am–12:15pm free-choice). Lunch (~1:00pm), rest hour (2:00– 3:00pm), afternoon activities (~3:15–5:15pm), dinner (~6:15pm), evening program (campfires,  games, etc.), lights out ~9:00–10:00pm by age. 

• Free-Choice Approach: Campers pick activities each day; no rigid sign-ups apart from riding  lessons and pre-registered trips (e.g., day hikes, overnights). Girls can roam camp to pursue tennis,  crafts, swimming, etc., at their own pace, promoting independence and self-directed goal-setting. Campers can freely move from one activity to another without any time restraints, as all activities  are open all day and therer are no activity periods. 

• Outdoor & Adventure Focus: Core wilderness trip offerings include hiking, backpacking, mountain  biking, rock climbing, and extensive whitewater paddling in nearby national forests. Campers build  foundational skills and progress to advanced trips over multiple summers. 

**CABINS & HOUSING** 

• Cabin Layout & Ratios: Most cabins house ~6 campers with 1 counselor (6:1 ratio). Overall staff-

to-camper ratio is about 1:4. Campers are grouped by age: Junior Line, Middle Line, Hillside Line,  and Senior Line. 

• Bath & Plumbing: All girl cabins have bathrooms (toilets, sinks, showers) inside. Campers do  basic cleaning; housekeeping handles deeper bathroom cleaning. 

**ATHLETIC & SPECIALTY ACTIVITIES** 

• Land Sports & Activities On-Site: Tennis courts, field games, gaga ball, arts & crafts, plus an  indoor lodge for rainy days. 

• Equestrian Program: Horseback riding lessons included in tuition. Campers can ride as often as  they like (up to 6 days/week in longer sessions). 

**OUTDOOR ADVENTURE** 

• Tripping Program: A century-long emphasis on wilderness skills. Options range from basic  overnights for younger campers to multi-day advanced backpacking, paddling, or climbing for  older/experienced girls. Whitewater paddling is a signature activity, with the chance for serious  rapids as skills increase. 

**RELIGIOUS & CULTURAL NOTES** 

• Non-Religious Camp: Not faith-based. A non-denominational Sunday “chapel” focuses on  reflection, friendship, and gratitude (no formal religious service). 

**DINING & FOOD ALLERGIES** 

• Meals: Served family-style with staff and campers at each table. Campers choose freely (e.g.,  peanut butter, salad bar). 

• Allergy Accommodations: Gluten, dairy, egg, soy, peanut, and tree nut allergies can generally be  managed if airborne peanut contact is not an issue. Peanut butter is present at a designated area. **HEALTH CENTER & SUPERVISION** 

• Medical Staffing: Two nurses on-site full-time; a rotating camp doctor (often a camp parent).  Nearest urgent care is ~15 minutes away, and parents are always called for significant issues. • Camper Supervision: Campers move freely among activities, but staff and line heads are  constantly circulating. A “missing camper drill” is performed every session, particularly for  waterfront safety. It should be noted that they do not have a uniform way to track camper  movements throughout the day and no real way of taking attendance. 

**LAUNDRY & TOWELS** 

• Laundry: Done weekly; quick turnaround. Campers do daily cabin chores (sweeping, tidying, etc.). **TRAVEL & TRANSPORTATION** 

• Drop-Off / Pick-Up: Most families drive for shorter sessions. Many 3- or 5-week campers fly  unaccompanied. Closest airports: Asheville (AVL) or Greenville-Spartanburg (GSP), with camp arranged shuttles. 

**CONTACT & FOLLOW-UP** 

• Families interested in learning more can visit the camp website or contact the camp office.  Directors are happy to speak directly with parents about session lengths, readiness for wilderness  trips, etc.

title: Green Cove (NC)
text:
***Overview*** 

Camp Green Cove is a specialized summer camp designed for neurodivergent children,  specifically those with ASD Level 1 (high-functioning autism), ADHD, and twice exceptional (2E) traits. The camp integrates a traditional day camp model with a strong  emphasis on providing a structured, supportive, and socially enriching environment. The  camp’s foundation stems from a partnership with **Camp Aquila**, a sleepaway camp  serving a similar population. 

***Camp Structure & Philosophy*** 

• **Small Group Sizes:** Each group consists of approximately **nine campers**, with a **3:1  camper-to-counselor ratio** to ensure individualized attention and social coaching. • **Highly Qualified Staff:** Counselors include **graduate students and college  students** studying fields related to neurodiversity. Unlike many camps that rely on  high school students as staff, Green Cove prioritizes counselors with a deeper  understanding of child development and intervention strategies. 

• **Leadership Team:** Includes **school psychologists, clinical social workers, and  special education teachers** who not only work directly with children but also  **mentor and supervise counselors** to maintain a high-quality support system. 

• **Slow-Paced, Structured Days:** The daily schedule is **designed to reduce  transitions** and anxiety. Children are provided with **previews of the day’s  schedule** and clear expectations to help them feel comfortable. 

***Activities & Programming*** 

Camp Green Cove offers a mix of structured and elective activities, balancing **preferred  and non-preferred activities** to encourage participation while accommodating individual  interests. 

• **Core Activity Offerings:** 

o **Sports:** Focused on movement, fun, and sportsmanship rather than  **competitive team-based skill development** (which is often less enjoyable  for this population). 

o **Performing Arts:** Includes **music, dance, and acting**, providing  

opportunities for self-expression in a non-judgmental environment.

o **Visual Arts & STEM:** Engages campers in **hands-on creative and scientific  projects**. 

o **Outdoor Play:** Non-competitive, movement-based games that allow  campers to explore social interactions in a structured way. 

• **Swimming Program:** 

o Takes place at **Iona College’s New Rochelle campus** (about **10 minutes  from the Bronxville campus**). 

o Campers are grouped into three categories: 

▪ Those wanting **swim skill development & safety instruction** 

▪ Those who **prefer free play in the water** 

▪ Those who are **hesitant about swimming but encouraged to engage  gradually** 

o Lifeguards will be present, and Green Cove staff will **join campers in the  water** for additional support. 

• **Daily Structure & Key Transitions:** 

o Campers arrive at **9:00 AM** via parent drop-off or centralized bus stops  (NYC’s East & West Side, Westchester, and possibly Greenwich). 

o The **day begins with group meetings**, all-camp singing, and a preview of the  schedule to **reduce anxiety**. 

o **Each activity lasts 45 minutes**, including both structured group activities  and individual electives. 

o **Snack and swim block (~2 hours):** Includes time for snack, travel to and  from the swim facility, and **40 minutes of pool time**. 

o **Lunch & Structured Free Play:** Campers bring their own lunch and can  choose between active or relaxing options afterward. 

o **Elective Activities (Afternoon):** Chosen **in advance by parents** to avoid in the-moment decision-making stress. 

o **Closing Circle:** Campers reflect on the day, ask questions about the next  day’s activities, and have a final snack before heading home. 

***Camper Eligibility & Admissions*** 

Camp Green Cove is designed for children who **fit a specific neurodivergent profile**.  While some may have **formal diagnoses (ASD Level 1, ADHD, 2E), others may not**, but all  must meet the following criteria: 

• **Age Range:** Entering **2nd through 7th grade** 

• **Cognitive Level:** At or **above grade level** academically

• **Behavioral Expectations:** 

o **Must be able to follow the structured camp schedule** with only mild  accommodations 

o **Cannot require one-on-one support** for extended periods 

o **No significant oppositional behaviors** (e.g., verbal/physical aggression) The **application process** includes: 

**1. Inquiry Form Submission** 

2\. **Questionnaires from Parents & Professionals** working with the child **3. Camper Assessment:** Either in-person at an **information session**, a **15-minute  Zoom call**, or a **direct observation if necessary** 

4\. **Final Decision:** Families are informed of acceptance after thorough review 

The camp places **strong emphasis on honesty** from families regarding their child’s needs  to **avoid a mismatch** in expectations. 

***Logistics & Enrollment*** 

• **Location:** Bronxville, NY (with swimming at Iona’s New Rochelle campus) • **Transportation:** 

o Centralized bus stops in **NYC (East Side, West Side), Westchester**, and  possibly **Greenwich** 

o Parent drop-off/pick-up is also an option 

• **Sessions & Capacity:** 

o Goal for **Summer 2024: 50 campers** (25 per session) 

o Maximum capacity in future summers: **120 campers** 

o Campers **must register for full sessions** (not week-by-week) for  consistency 

• **Meals & Snacks:** 

o **Morning and afternoon snacks are provided (nut-free & kosher)** o **Campers bring their own lunch** to ensure familiarity and avoid food-related  anxiety 

***Conflict Resolution & Camper Support*** 

• **Social coaching is a key part of the camp experience.** Counselors are trained to  intervene **in real-time** when campers struggle with social interactions, helping  them navigate misunderstandings or frustrations.

• **Proactive behavior management** is emphasized, ensuring that potential conflicts  or sensitivities are **anticipated rather than simply reacted to**. 

• **Zen Den:** A **sensory-friendly space** is available for campers who need time to  regroup. 

• **Daily communication with parents:** Division heads check in **weekly** but often  communicate **daily** via calls or texts. 

***Upcoming Information Sessions & Outreach*** 

• **February 9:** In-person **open house at The Shefa School, NYC** 

• **March (Date TBD):** Another in-person **open house** 

• **End of February:** Virtual **"Chat with Me" Lunch Session** for parents • Additional information sessions may be added based on interest.

title: Forest Lake Recap (1)
text:
**Forest Lake Camp — Partner Call Recap (Geoff Blanck, Michael Childs, Coby Porter)** 

**Quick positioning** 

● **Brother–sister camp** (boys’ camp + girls’ camp on the same property). 

● **Not co-ed for activities** (activities are single-gender), but the camps **come together** for: 

○ Sunday campfire + special events (SPEV), play, etc. 

○ **Brother–sister time on Sundays** for siblings. 

● Strong emphasis on **child development** over “babysitting/entertainment,” in a **low-pressure, supportive environment** (not highly competitive). 

**Leadership & key people** 

● **Geoff Blanck**: Owner (bought camp ~17 years ago; 4th generation Confer family did not continue). Deep personal/alumni connection (he attended; his father attended; his children now staff). 

● **Michael Childs**: New **Summer Camp Director** (formerly director of **Camp Lakota** through 2024). 

○ Philosophy: **“Every camper, every day”** (staff focus on ensuring every child is seen/supported daily). 

● **Coby Porter**: Marketing/sales lead (hired ~1.5 years ago) focused on growing the **new camper funnel**. 

**What’s changed / what they want from Camp Experts** 

● Geoff explicitly said **referral relationships lapsed** due to **top leadership turnover** in recent years (they own it), and they want to **rebuild communication + referrals**. 

● Camp is strong and stable; they’ve reinvested heavily and want to be reintroduced to families.

**Size, feel, and capacity** 

● **Small camp by design**: ~**90 boys + 90 girls** at full capacity at one time. 

● Guiding principle: leadership should **know every camper’s name**—otherwise they’re “too big.” 

● Cabin structure: **8–9 cabins** per boys/girls side; **10–12 campers per cabin**, typically **single age group**. 

● **LIT program** (age ~16): capped at **8 boys + 8 girls**; has been filling and sometimes turning kids away; used as a staff pipeline. 

● Enrollment pattern: **Session 1 typically sold out**, **Session 2 lighter** (common industry challenge). 

**Campus / differentiators** 

● **823 acres** in the Adirondacks, **private lake**, **25+ miles of trails**. 

● They’re leaning into land-based differentiators to keep **13–15-year-olds** engaged (who often peel off to specialty programs): 

○ **Mountain biking**: increasingly a “core” experience; building more trails; planning a **pump track**; ~**16 newer bikes** + strong returning instructor. 

**Program structure & sessions** 

● Camp runs **7 weeks** total. 

● “Full session” options: **4-week** or **3-week** sessions. 

● **2-week trial option** for **first-year campers** (no age cutoff). 

○ Often kids want to **extend**; they ask families to keep calendars open if beds are available. 

● Additional options mentioned: 

○ **5-week** option (arrive week 3 through end)

○ **7-week** full summer 

● **Family Camp**: “week 8” (after main camp) — full-week family program that acts as a **recruitment pipeline** for future campers; they said they can **pay referral fees** for family camp referrals too. 

**Camper fit (“who thrives here”)** 

● Best for kids/families who want: 

○ **Supportive** environment to **try new things / try on a new self** 

○ Growth in **confidence, belonging, and participation** 

● Geoff gave two classic “fit” examples: 

○ “Popular athlete” who wouldn’t try drama at home → can safely do it here. 

○ Kid who wouldn’t make a school team → here they **participate, improve, and are rewarded for effort**. 

● Not framed as a “win-every-game” culture; more about **effort, sportsmanship, and development**. 

**Camper geography / demographics** 

● Historically (pre-COVID): roughly **1/3 tri-state**, **1/3 rest of U.S. (20 states)**, **1/3 international**. 

● Now: **international down significantly** (previously strong South America/Venezuela presence); they’re working to rebuild. 

● Current pockets: tri-state + Boston area; also pockets in **Texas, Florida, Chicago, Ohio**, etc. 

● Many “remote” geographies tend to be **alumni-connected**. 

**Transportation**

● Mentioned airports include **JFK and Albany**; they said they try to **accommodate transportation needs** “to the best of their ability” (within reason). 

**Signature additions / “news”** 

● **Intersession Trip (new-ish, now ongoing):** 

○ Runs during the break between the **4-week** and **3-week** sessions. 

○ For campers staying through intersession (5- or 7-week kids): bus trip to **Lake Placid**: 

■ Camp out, hike (one of the “46ers”), adventure park/Alpine slide, Olympic site tour, Mirror Lake, dinner, camp again, return as new session arrives. 

○ Goal: make intersession exciting + encourage extensions (“jealousy effect”). 

**Activities & extra fees (important details)** 

● **Waterskiing** no longer offered (liability/outfitters exiting the market). 

● Replacement: **Lake George powerboat cruise + tubing + cliff** 

**jumping/snorkeling/island exploring** (approx **$130** quoted for a 2–3 hour trip; kids often want multiple trips). 

● Other extra-fee add-ons: 

○ **Whitewater rafting** on the Hudson (**full-day** for regular campers; **half-day** for family camp) with lunch provided. 

○ **Horseback riding** (extra fee; ~**50%** participate). Program includes ~**12 horses**, two rings, pasture, trail rides, and an **overnight horseback trip** (ride out, camp at an outpost, ride back). 

○ **New for mountain biking:** planned **overnight bike trip** (pack/ride/camp/return). 

**What to emphasize when matching families** 

● Families seeking:

○ Small, name-known community 

○ Balanced traditional camp + **big-land Adirondack adventure** ○ Confidence-building, “try something new” culture 

○ Strong leadership values (“every camper, every day”) 

● Good for teens who might otherwise drift to specialty camps—especially **mountain bike/outdoors** kids.

title: Gateway Ed
text:
**Snapshot** 

Gateway operates the Cambridge-based academic summer program at Queens’ College, built around small-group Oxbridge-style teaching, hands-on learning, and selective cohorts. Their model combines morning academic tutorials with afternoon practical sessions, producing strong university-prep outcomes at a more accessible price point than typical pre-college programs. 

**Location & Housing (Queens’ College, Cambridge)** ● Program fully housed within Queens’ College. 

● Students live in the Cripps Building, the most modern and recently renovated residence on campus. 

● Rooms are ensuite or ensuite-plus-one. 

● Daily meals are taken in the main dining hall; each cohort ends with a graduation dinner in the historic formal hall. 

● Oxford location exists, but is available only for closed groups this year. 

**Ages, Cohorts & Structure** 

● Ages 14–17. 

● Each cohort runs 13 days (two weeks). 

● Three subject fields offered each session. 

● Up to 20 students per subject; approximately 60 students per cohort. **Academic Model**

**Morning Tutorials** 

● Oxbridge-style academic sessions in small groups. 

● Led by subject experts with a focus on higher-level critical thinking. 

**Afternoon Hands-On Component** 

Medicine: 

● Emergency medicine concepts, cardiovascular systems, and clinical reasoning. ● Heart and kidney dissection. 

● Work with advanced simulation mannequins that speak, cough, and display symptoms for diagnosis. 

Business & Finance: 

● Students build a product concept with budgeting, packaging, branding, marketing, and logistics. 

● Final project serves as an academic artifact for applications. 

Computer Science & AI: 

● Rising-demand track. 

● Students must bring laptops (no tablets/iPads). 

● Some content does not function on iOS. 

**Student Outcomes** 

● Letter of recommendation from an industry expert. 

● Final project tailored to their field of study.

● Meaningful additions for applications to Russell Group, Ivy League, and other selective universities. 

**English Requirements & Admissions** 

● Minimum English level B2. 

● Video-based screening tool used to verify proficiency and detect scripted responses. ● Nationality cap of around five students per subject to maintain balance. ● Friends may request to take the same subject together. 

**Activities, Evenings & Supervision** 

● Approximately 1:15 staffing ratio with four staff on-site. 

● Evening activities include football, outdoor games, and board games. ● Consistent structure to keep students engaged throughout the day. 

**Excursions (Two Full Days Weekly)** 

Examples include: 

● London walking tour (Big Ben, Tower Bridge, London Eye). 

● Oxford walking tour. 

● Warwick Castle. 

● Hampton Court Palace.

● Cambridge punting and other local experiences. 

**Travel & Airport Transfers** 

● Heathrow and Gatwick supported. 

● Transfers included in program price. 

● Three daily pickup windows: morning, afternoon, and (if necessary) evening. 

**Fit for Families** 

Ideal for students who: 

● Want academic challenge with practical exposure. 

● Are exploring Medicine, Business/Finance, or Computer Science/AI. 

● Are interested in a genuine Cambridge college residential experience. ● Want tangible academic outputs to support future university applications. ● Prefer balanced international cohorts rather than programs dominated by one nationality. 

**Pricing & Early Bird** 

● Program priced below university-branded Oxbridge summer schools. ● Early bird deadline set for 31 January (website update pending at time of call). ● Advisors should reconfirm current pricing before quoting families.

**How to Present Gateway to Parents** 

● Cambridge college setting with modern ensuite housing. 

● Academic depth combined with real-world hands-on learning. ● Strong student outcomes: recommendation letter + project portfolio. ● Balanced international mix due to nationality caps and English screening. ● Strong excursions included every week. 

● Good value relative to competitors.

title: FC Como 1907
text:
**1. Introduction & Context** 

• **Participants & Setting:** 

o The meeting is conducted via a video call, with Juliette Bolon, Chiara Aliverti,  and Sam Goldberg as the primary speakers. 

o The conversation centers on reviewing and explaining the official website for  the Como 1907 summer camps. 

• **Purpose:** 

o To walk through the website and provide a detailed explanation of the two  types of camp programs offered, as well as to address questions regarding  application processes, logistics, and promotional materials. 

**2. Camp Programs Overview** 

• **Division into Two Programs:** 

o The Como 1907 summer camps are split into two distinct programs: ▪ **Elite Performance Camp** 

▪ **Discovery Summer Camp** 

• **Camp Duration & Dates:** 

o The overall camp period spans 6 weeks, running from June 20 to August 2. 

**3. Elite Performance Camp Details** 

• **Target Audience & Duration:** 

o Geared toward both boys and girls aged 16 to 18. 

o This is a 2-week intensive program. 

• **Focus & Training Environment:** 

o The camp is described as a high-level, preseason preparation program for  serious football (soccer) enthusiasts. 

o It is positioned as a professional, personalized experience meant to not only  enhance skills but also to boost the applicants’ profiles for future university  or college applications. 

• **Facility & Training Grounds:** 

o Training takes place at the professional Mozza training grounds – the same  facilities used by the Como 1907 Serie A team.

o Additional benefits include: 

▪ Access to a state-of-the-art gym. 

▪ Video analysis sessions where players receive detailed feedback. ▪ Interaction with academy teams (including training sessions with the  Primavera team, which represents the under-21 squad). 

• **Application Process:** 

o Applicants must submit a personal statement, a letter of recommendation  from their current coach, highlights video or reel, and a sports resume. o The selection process is overseen by key figures such as Osha Roberts (Head  of Development) and Rafael Varane (a prominent member of the Como 1907  Development Committee). 

• **Capacity & Gender Split:** 

o The maximum number of participants is set at 60, ideally split evenly  between 30 boys and 30 girls. 

o In instances where numbers are unbalanced, adjustments will be made  using academy players to maintain a robust camp environment. 

• **Additional Camp Components:** 

o **Side Activities & Cultural Immersion:** 

▪ On the first Wednesday of the program, players have a day off  

dedicated to discovering Como. 

▪ Activities include a private boat tour on Como Lake, a guided visit to  Bellagio, and a tour of local attractions (such as a villa in Bellagio with  its gardens). 

o **Closing Ceremony:** 

▪ Held at the Senegalia Stadium where parents and legal guardians are  invited. 

▪ Players receive certificates of attendance with detailed feedback  based on video analysis and coach evaluations. 

o **Uniform & Gear:** 

▪ Participants receive a complete training kit (shorts, t-shirts, socks,  sweatshirts, trousers, a cap, water bottle, backpack, and a football)  

bearing the Como 1907 logo. 

**4. Discovery Summer Camp Details** 

• **Target Audience & Structure:** 

o Designed for children aged 9 to 16.

o The program is structured over 4 weeks and is divided into age-specific  groups (for example, one week for 9-10-year-olds). 

o Both boys and girls are welcome. 

• **Program Focus & Activities:** 

o **Football Training:** 

▪ Morning sessions consist of 2 to 2.5 hours of football training  

(focused on skill development but with a lower entry barrier—no  

previous football experience required). 

o **Cultural & Recreational Activities:** 

▪ Afternoons include a variety of activities such as: 

• Exploring Como City Center. 

• Boat tours on the lake. 

• Visits to local landmarks. 

• A cooking class where kids learn to prepare Italian dishes. 

• Game or bowling nights, hiking, and even a lesson in martial  

arts. 

o **Cultural Immersion:** 

▪ Emphasis is placed on total immersion in Italian culture—not just on  the football field. 

▪ Small Italian language classes are also offered to introduce basic  Italian phrases and cultural nuances. 

• **Accommodation & Board:** 

o Participants stay in double rooms with full board treatment—breakfast,  lunch, and dinner are included. 

• **Capacity:** 

o The maximum capacity for each week is 16 kids, ensuring a more intimate  and focused camp experience. 

**5. Staffing, Supervision, and Safety** 

• **Counselor & Coach Details:** 

o Counselors are recruited through a specialist agency in Italy. 

o The age requirement for counselors is set at 24 years and older, ensuring  maturity and experience. 

o All counselors and coaches are police-checked (using the equivalent of DBS  checks) and are first-aid trained.

• **Camper-to-Staff Ratios:** 

o For children up to 12 years old, the ratio is 1:6. 

o For older campers, the ratio is 1:10, which complies with Italian legal  requirements. 

• **Hotel Supervision:** 

o The residence hotel is run by a long-standing local Italian family. 

o A dedicated chef (who also provides cooking lessons) and support staff are  on hand to assist, ensuring that both training and off-field activities are  safely managed. 

**6. Promotional Materials and Outreach** 

• **Digital & Print Resources:** 

o Juliette mentions the creation of a digital leaflet, posters for schools, and a  video reel showcasing the camp facilities and activities. 

o These materials are intended to assist in marketing the camp and are  available for distribution upon request. 

• **Geographic Targeting:** 

o While the program is new, the target is to attract a diverse international  audience—with a focus on the United States, though participants from  various regions are welcomed. 

• **Communication & Follow-Up:** 

o Monica (from the Camp Experts team) is responsible for handling leads and  passing on details to Chiara once families are ready to be contacted directly. o The official email address for the summer camp is provided on the website  and used for all further communications. 

**7. Q&A and Additional Insights** 

• **Questions Addressed:** 

o Details about the application process, capacity (60 kids maximum for the  elite program and 16 per week for the discovery camps), and gender  balance. 

o Discussion about the level of professionalism in the elite camp compared to  the more relaxed, cultural immersion focus of the discovery camps.

o Inquiries regarding staffing, including the age and qualifications of  counselors and the legal requirements (police checks, first aid certification,  etc.). 

o How the camp will manage logistics such as accommodation, meals, and  the integration of cultural activities. 

• **Emphasis on Quality and Experience:** 

o Both speakers stress that the elite performance camp is designed to provide  a high-level, professional environment—using first-team facilities and  integrating training with practical exposure to the Como 1907 academy. 

o The discovery camp, on the other hand, is presented as a well-rounded,  immersive experience for younger children that combines football training  with the rich cultural offerings of the Como region. 

• **Future Steps:** 

o With the website recently launched, enrollment numbers are still in their  infancy (only one sign-up at the time of the meeting), but the team is  enthusiastic about building momentum. 

o The speakers express their commitment to supporting prospective families  with additional promotional materials and answering any further questions. 

**8. Conclusion** 

• **Final Remarks:** 

o Sam Goldberg thanks the team for the detailed walk-through and expresses  excitement about starting to send kids to the camps. 

o The meeting wraps up with mutual agreement to share additional  promotional resources and to remain available for any further inquiries. • **Next Actions:** 

o Distribution of digital materials and the official contact email. 

o Follow-up on any leads, particularly the inquiry about a potential camper  from Switzerland. 

o Continued collaboration among the team members to ensure smooth  communication and successful enrollment.

title: Eastwood (Zoom - Nov. 4th)
text:
**Eastwood – Call Recap**  

Internal summary for Camp Experts | Program: Eastwood (K–4 short-session overnight) | Prepared: Nov 7, 2025 

**Program Overview (What We Heard)** 

Eastwood is a new, purpose-built short-session overnight program for rising K–4 campers. Located on the former Ghost Light property adjacent to Camp Manitou and near Camp Somerset (Belgrade Lakes, ME), the site is compact with clear sightlines and a central pool. Sessions are one or two weeks, designed as discrete offerings (no in-camp bridges), with the aim of building confidence and readiness for a ‘forever camp’ later on. Directors: Lauren Beckwith (former Manitou program director, elementary educator) and Stacey Levy (Somerset founding team, PE/health educator), both long-time camp professionals and parents. JD provides executive support. 

**Who It’s For (Fit Signals)** 

• Early elementary campers (K–4) ready for a first overnight in a small, structured environment. 

• Younger siblings of Manitou/Somerset campers or families with strong camp affinity among teachers/coaches. 

• Families vacationing in Maine seeking a contained, joyful overnight experience (not day camp). 

• Parents who value explicit social-emotional and executive-function skill building, not just ‘mini-camp’. 

• Campers who would benefit from shorter runway before 3.5–7 week traditional programs. **Program & Philosophy (Design Signals)** 

Three daily morning blocks and three afternoon blocks balance instruction and play. Four core areas: Athletics (team/individual), STEAM (strong emphasis), Adventure & Movement, and Aquatics (instructional and recreational swim). Intentional play modalities include open play (recess-style), guided play, and elective-style choice periods. Values are taught explicitly and mapped to ‘forest friends’ (e.g., Bear=Bravery, Otter=Play/Friendship, Deer=Kindness/Community, Owl=Care/Impact) to make expectations concrete for K–4. 

**Facilities & Access** 

• Eastwood campus includes pool, central green, large field; new multi-sport court planned (pickleball, street hockey, basketball). 

• Supplemental access (as available) to Manitou/Somerset waterfronts, Manitou ropes, and Somerset gymnastics ‘Somersault’ on their off-periods/trip days. 

• A property path to lake access is being developed to avoid crossing main camp when needed. **Operations & Policies** 

• Cabins are single-gender and by grade; target ~100 campers per session at full buildout (intentionally smaller than M/S).

• Travel: buses from NYC/CT and Boston; optional Maine meet-up. No arrival-day parent drop-off; parents may tour with their camper at pickup. 

• Communication: no phone calls during session; Campanion photo/email plus letters (‘old-school mail’). 

• Uniform-lite: a few Eastwood tees to support belonging at community events. 

• Support: eligibility criteria to ensure readiness; case-by-case for campers on the spectrum. Social work background on staff (Stacey). 

**Sessions, Capacity & Early Signals** 

Sessions: a one-week option (calendar-driven this year) plus two-week sessions; families may mix sessions across the summer, but sessions are discrete (no in-camp bridging). Early interest appears strongest in the late■July window; current enrollments are beginning with K–2. Capacity allows for ~100 campers per session with utilities in place for eventual cabin expansion if demand warrants. 

**Open Questions / Watchouts** 

• Enrollment curve: as a short-session new launch, decision timing may skew later (Q1). Monitor pace by grade. 

• One-week vs two-week: first-year utility test; late July appears strongest. Track demand by region. 

• Support bandwidth: clarity on thresholds for higher-support campers as momentum builds. 

• Parent expectations: reiterate no arrival-day drop-off and no phone calls during session to avoid mismatch. 

**Our Impressions**  

• Positioning is clear and credible: an overnight bridge that’s small, structured, and joyful—built for success at the next camp. 

• Curriculum-level thoughtfulness (values, play modalities, executive-function skills) is unusually explicit for K–4 and should resonate with parents seeking developmental intentionality. 

• Access to Manitou/Somerset is a strength, yet Eastwood’s standalone design avoids ‘satellite camp’ vibes. 

• Expect healthy pull from sibling networks and Maine vacationers 

**Notables to Keep Handy with Families** 

• Ages/grades K–4; single-gender cabins by grade; ~100 per session target. • Sessions are discrete (no bridges); families can mix sessions across the summer. • Buses from NYC/CT/BOS; Maine meet-up option; parents tour at pickup, not arrival. • No phone calls during session; Campanion plus letters for communications.

• Uniform-lite (a few Eastwood tees). 

**Light Org Notes** 

• Best-tool fit: younger siblings, counselor/teacher families, EF-focused parents, and Maine travelers. 

• If a family requests a short, intentional launch toward a longer camp, this aligns squarely. 

**Contact & Next Steps** 

Directors: Lauren Beckwith and Stacey Levy. For detailed dates/rates or to confirm current availability by session and grade, coordinate with Eastwood and note bus origin preferences early. Keep this recap accessible for family conversations; it pairs well with Somerset/Manitou discussions when siblings are involved.

title: Eagle Island Zoom Recap
text:
**Camp Schedule & Activities** 

The daily schedule is structured to provide **a mix of group-based and choice-based  activities**, offering opportunities for campers to **develop skills, explore nature, and build  friendships**. 

**Typical Daily Schedule (For 9–11-Year-Olds)** 

**1. 8:00 AM – Breakfast:** 

a. Campers **eat with their assigned “grove”** (housing group). 

b. Each **grove has a dedicated counselor** to foster connection and  community. 

**2. Morning Activities:** 

a. Campers choose **three specialized activities** on their first day, which they  repeat throughout the week for **progressive learning**. 

**3. 12:00 PM – Lunch:** 

a. Campers return to their **grove group** for a shared meal. 

b. Counselors **actively listen** for any concerns or highlights from the morning’s  activities. 

**4. Waterfront Activities:** 

a. **Mandatory swim lessons** (water safety is a priority due to the island  setting). 

b. Free swim and **boating (kayaks, canoes, “corkles” - round paddle boats)**. c. Advanced campers can opt for **sailing or in-depth paddling lessons**. **5. Rest Hour & “Quests” (Community Tasks):** 

a. Campers participate in island upkeep through small **chores (“quests”)**,  reinforcing responsibility and teamwork. 

**6. Dinner:** 

a. Campers regroup with their **groves** for another meal together. 

**7. Evening Programs:** 

a. A **variety of rotating evening activities**, including group games, storytelling,  and performances.

**Activity Offerings** 

Campers have **core activities** offered each week, along with rotating options that **change  week to week**. 

***Core Activities (Available Every Week)*** 

• **Nature Education:** Environmental and ecological learning experiences. • **Sailing:** A key waterfront activity, with lessons available. 

• **Boating:** Kayaking, canoeing, and other paddle sports. 

• **Camp Skills:** Fire-building, orienteering, outdoor survival. 

• **Arts & Crafts:** A mix of creative and hands-on projects. 

• **Music, Dance, & Drama:** Includes improv games, songwriting, and skits. • **Camp Newspaper:** Campers can participate in writing and publishing. 

***Rotating Activities (Change Weekly)*** 

• Guest instructors from **Project Music** or **The Loon Center**. 

• **Chess**, **woodworking**, or specialized art projects. 

Campers attending for multiple weeks will **always have new activities** to choose from. 

**Program Length & Housing Accommodations** 

Eagle Island offers **one-week and two-week programs**, with campers able to **extend their  stay up to six weeks**. 

**Housing Accommodations** 

• **One-week campers** stay in **historic buildings** (**Loon Lodge & The Annex**), which  have **electricity and bunk beds**. 

• **Two-week campers** stay in **rustic tents or cabins**, both with **four beds per unit**. • **All housing is close to modern, fully equipped bathrooms with electricity and  flush toilets** (a “glamping” style setup).

***Weekend Stays (For Campers Attending Consecutive Sessions)*** 

• Campers staying for multiple weeks can **remain on the island over the weekend**,  participating in **special weekend-only activities**, such as: 

o **Voting on and cooking a meal over a campfire.** 

o **“Overnight Campouts”** in different areas of the island (waterfront,  peninsula, etc.). 

o **Board game nights** or **capture-the-flag-style games (Predator Prey)**. 

**Teen Leadership Programs (Ages 15–17)** 

For older campers, Eagle Island offers **specialized leadership programs**: 

• **Lifeguard-in-Training (LIT):** Campers earn **waterfront lifeguarding certification**. • **Skippers Program:** An **intensive sailing program** for advanced water skills. • **Junior Leadership Program (CIT Alternative):** 

o Training in **team leadership, resume writing, and job skills**. 

o Campers meet **college recruiters** and learn how to **leverage camp  experience** for future employment. 

**Meals & Dining** 

Eagle Island partners with **Greek House Chefs**, providing **fresh, high-quality meals**. 

• **Breakfast bar with multiple options.** 

• **Salad bar available at lunch and dinner.** 

• **Individual meal accommodations for dietary restrictions.** 

• **Kitchen staff will contact families to discuss specific dietary needs.** 

**Safety & Medical Care** 

• **1:8 camper-to-staff ratio.** 

• **Two-week staff training program**, including emergency response. • **On-site nurse available at all times.**

• **CPR & first aid trained staff spread across camp.** 

• **20-minute transport to Adirondack Medical Center in case of emergency.** • **Fire rescue boat docked at mainland dock, with trained personnel familiar with  the island.** 

**Camper Communication** 

Eagle Island is an **unplugged camp**, meaning campers do not use phones or digital  devices. 

• **Parents may call camp for updates.** Staff will check on the camper and respond. • **Campers can send & receive letters via mail** (recommended to be sent three days  in advance). 

• **One-way email system (“Campgrams”)**: Parents can email campers, staff prints  and distributes them. 

• **In special cases, scanned camper letters may be sent back home.** 

**Camp Enrollment & Financial Aid** 

• **74 camper capacity per session** (most sessions are half full, some are at waitlist  capacity). 

• **One-week and two-week sessions are the most popular**. 

• **35% of campers receive financial assistance**. 

o **Scholarships available on a first-come, first-served basis**. 

o No tax returns required; just a short questionnaire. 

**Special Notes** 

• **Campers with medical conditions (e.g., diabetes, severe allergies) will be  evaluated case-by-case**. 

• **The camp is ideal for first-time campers**, those looking to **try a variety of  activities**, and those **seeking outdoor adventure**. 

• **Not a fit for campers seeking specific skill-focused training (e.g., formal sports,  music, or theater instruction).**

**Why Choose Eagle Island?** 

**Key Differentiators** 

1\. **Private Island Experience** – Complete camp immersion with no external visitors. 2. **Ideal First-Time Camp** – Short sessions, bridge programs, and staff trained in  homesickness support. 

3\. **Unplugged Nature Immersion** – Campers learn to engage with nature, social  interactions, and outdoor skills. 

4\. **Leadership Development for Teens** – Practical skill-building for future careers. 5. **Choice-Based Learning** – Campers **customize** their experience based on personal  interests. 

**Final Notes from the Presentation** 

• Eagle Island Camp is currently in **its 5th year of revitalization**. 

• The camp intends to **expand offerings, including a parent weekend for six-week  campers** in future summers. 

• Most **camp inquiries come from families in the Northeast (Manhattan, Brooklyn,  New Jersey, and upstate NY)**. 

• Families are encouraged to **register early** for **financial aid and preferred session  dates**.

title: Culver
text:
**Culver Summer Schools & Camps — Camp Experts Partner Recap** 

**The quick take** 

Culver is a **legacy leadership-driven summer program** on a massive boarding school campus in Indiana, running a **single, immersive 6-week session** for **~1,300 campers** ages **9–17**. It blends **classic camp, structured leadership development, and academics/enrichment**, with two distinct experiences: **Woodcraft Camp (ages 9–14)** and **Upper Camp (ages 14–17)**. 

**What Culver is** 

● **Founded: 1902** (120+ year legacy) 

● **Core philosophy: learning + leading through experience**, responsibility, and shared values 

● **Why it’s different:** scale, tradition, and a **servant-leadership model** using a military-style framework (organizational, not boot camp) 

**Location & campus** 

● **North-central Indiana**, ~**2 hours from Chicago** and **2 hours from Indianapolis** 

● **1,800-acre campus** with **1 mile of private lakefront** on Lake Maxinkuckee (second-largest natural lake in Indiana) 

● Summer campers use the **same facilities as the academic-year boarding school** 

**Program structure (important for placement)** **Woodcraft Camp**

● **Ages: 9–14** 

● **Setting:** rustic, traditional camp 

● **Housing:** A-frame cabins, **12 campers per cabin**, bathhouses, no A/C ● **Capacity: ~780 campers**, ~**220 staff** 

● **Overall ratio:** ~**1:4** 

● **Accreditation:** ACA accredited 

**Upper Camp** 

● **Ages: 14–17** 

● **Setting:** boarding-school style 

● **Housing:** dorms & barracks with **A/C and indoor plumbing** 

● **Group size:** girls ~35, boys ~45 per unit, **4 counselors per group** ● **Capacity:** ~**500–550 campers** 

**Key note:** 

Both programs run **simultaneously for the same 6-week dates**, making Culver ideal for **siblings across age groups**. 

**Session length & calendar** 

● **One session only: 6 weeks** 

● All campers (Woodcraft + Upper) attend for the **full duration** 

● Nearly **1,300 campers on campus at once**

**Daily life & academics/enrichment** 

**Woodcraft (ages 9–14)** 

● Campers choose **5 elective classes** at a time 

● Classes rotate in **10-day trimesters** 

● **80+ electives**, including: 

○ sailing, horsemanship 

○ air rifle, archery 

○ sports skills, golf 

○ creative/novelty classes (e.g., Rubik’s Cube “speed cubing”) 

● **Mixed-age cabins** (within 9–12 and 12–14 bands) used intentionally to teach **followership → leadership** 

**Upper Camp (ages 14–17)** 

● **6 classes per day, Monday–Friday** 

● **Boys choose a “major”:** sailing, aviation, or horsemanship 

● **Girls do not declare majors**, but may take all the same classes 

● Additional electives include: 

○ waterskiing 

○ rifle 

○ academic classes (SAT/ACT prep) 

○ **independent study** for schoolwork from home 

● **Technology-free** for the full 6 weeks (phones collected at check-in, returned final night)

**Leadership model (the heart of Culver)** **Shared values** 

Every camper and staff member carries an **Honor Card** with Culver’s five values: ● honesty 

● leadership 

● cooperation 

● self-discipline 

● consideration 

**Woodcraft leadership** 

● Military-style **organizational framework** (not boot camp) 

● Weekly leadership positions assigned through: 

○ observation of values in action 

○ interviews 

○ peer followership feedback 

● Culminates in earning a **C-PIN**, the symbolic goal of Woodcraft ● Emphasis on **learning to follow well before leading** 

**Upper Camp leadership** 

● Weekly leadership “watches” with formal **change-of-watch ceremonies** ● Leadership roles decided via interviews and reflections 

● Includes: 

○ leadership binders passed between outgoing/incoming officers

○ seminars on goal-setting and self-awareness 

○ unit-level leadership + campus-wide roles 

**Visible tradition** 

● **Weekly Saturday parade** with all ~1,300 campers in uniform 

● Horses, marching units, and **aviation flyover** 

**Supervision, discipline & support** 

**Cabin & dorm supervision** 

● Woodcraft: **9 staff per 60 campers** in each unit (1 counselor sleeps in-cabin) ● Upper Camp: counselors live directly in dorms/barracks with campers 

**Behavior & discipline** 

● Discipline framed as **learning and growth**, not punishment 

● Interventions first; separation only if a camper cannot re-engage ● Some campers placed on **disciplinary contracts** as structured second chances 

**Emotional Support Services (ESS)** 

● **Year-round licensed counselors & social workers** 

● Two health centers in summer (main campus + Woodcraft) 

● Required counseling for some campers based on disclosed needs ● ESS staff embedded in daily life; strong support for: 

○ homesickness

○ anxiety 

○ transitions 

● Camp may **decline placement** if needs exceed what Culver can responsibly support 

**Community & culture notes** 

● **Co-ed programs**, but cabins assigned by **sex assigned at birth** 

● No gender-neutral cabins; classes and activities are co-ed 

● **English is the official language** in public spaces 

● Campers from **40+ states and ~30 countries** 

**Parent access & visitation** 

● **Two permit weekends** during the summer: 

○ after week 2 

○ after week 4 

● Parents may sign campers out during the day and return them by evening 

**Transportation** 

● **Chicago O’Hare (ORD)** is the designated airport 

● Culver runs **12–15 charter buses** for arrivals and departures 

● Families simply book flights to/from O’Hare

**Positioning / who thrives here** 

**Best fit for:** 

● kids ready for **structure, routine, and accountability** 

● families seeking **deep character + leadership development** ● campers who benefit from **technology-free immersion** 

● students who may later reference Culver meaningfully in **college essays** 

**Important to flag for families:** 

● long **6-week commitment only** 

● strong structure and expectations 

● not a casual or drop-in camp experience 

● leadership growth may involve **discomfort before confidence**

title: Culver
text:
**Culver Summer Schools & Camps — Camp Experts Partner Recap** 

**The quick take** 

Culver is a **legacy leadership-driven summer program** on a massive boarding school campus in Indiana, running a **single, immersive 6-week session** for **~1,300 campers** ages **9–17**. It blends **classic camp, structured leadership development, and academics/enrichment**, with two distinct experiences: **Woodcraft Camp (ages 9–14)** and **Upper Camp (ages 14–17)**. 

**What Culver is** 

● **Founded: 1902** (120+ year legacy) 

● **Core philosophy: learning + leading through experience**, responsibility, and shared values 

● **Why it’s different:** scale, tradition, and a **servant-leadership model** using a military-style framework (organizational, not boot camp) 

**Location & campus** 

● **North-central Indiana**, ~**2 hours from Chicago** and **2 hours from Indianapolis** 

● **1,800-acre campus** with **1 mile of private lakefront** on Lake Maxinkuckee (second-largest natural lake in Indiana) 

● Summer campers use the **same facilities as the academic-year boarding school** 

**Program structure (important for placement)** **Woodcraft Camp**

● **Ages: 9–14** 

● **Setting:** rustic, traditional camp 

● **Housing:** A-frame cabins, **12 campers per cabin**, bathhouses, no A/C ● **Capacity: ~780 campers**, ~**220 staff** 

● **Overall ratio:** ~**1:4** 

● **Accreditation:** ACA accredited 

**Upper Camp** 

● **Ages: 14–17** 

● **Setting:** boarding-school style 

● **Housing:** dorms & barracks with **A/C and indoor plumbing** 

● **Group size:** girls ~35, boys ~45 per unit, **4 counselors per group** ● **Capacity:** ~**500–550 campers** 

**Key note:** 

Both programs run **simultaneously for the same 6-week dates**, making Culver ideal for **siblings across age groups**. 

**Session length & calendar** 

● **One session only: 6 weeks** 

● All campers (Woodcraft + Upper) attend for the **full duration** 

● Nearly **1,300 campers on campus at once**

**Daily life & academics/enrichment** 

**Woodcraft (ages 9–14)** 

● Campers choose **5 elective classes** at a time 

● Classes rotate in **10-day trimesters** 

● **80+ electives**, including: 

○ sailing, horsemanship 

○ air rifle, archery 

○ sports skills, golf 

○ creative/novelty classes (e.g., Rubik’s Cube “speed cubing”) 

● **Mixed-age cabins** (within 9–12 and 12–14 bands) used intentionally to teach **followership → leadership** 

**Upper Camp (ages 14–17)** 

● **6 classes per day, Monday–Friday** 

● **Boys choose a “major”:** sailing, aviation, or horsemanship 

● **Girls do not declare majors**, but may take all the same classes 

● Additional electives include: 

○ waterskiing 

○ rifle 

○ academic classes (SAT/ACT prep) 

○ **independent study** for schoolwork from home 

● **Technology-free** for the full 6 weeks (phones collected at check-in, returned final night)

**Leadership model (the heart of Culver)** **Shared values** 

Every camper and staff member carries an **Honor Card** with Culver’s five values: ● honesty 

● leadership 

● cooperation 

● self-discipline 

● consideration 

**Woodcraft leadership** 

● Military-style **organizational framework** (not boot camp) 

● Weekly leadership positions assigned through: 

○ observation of values in action 

○ interviews 

○ peer followership feedback 

● Culminates in earning a **C-PIN**, the symbolic goal of Woodcraft ● Emphasis on **learning to follow well before leading** 

**Upper Camp leadership** 

● Weekly leadership “watches” with formal **change-of-watch ceremonies** ● Leadership roles decided via interviews and reflections 

● Includes: 

○ leadership binders passed between outgoing/incoming officers

○ seminars on goal-setting and self-awareness 

○ unit-level leadership + campus-wide roles 

**Visible tradition** 

● **Weekly Saturday parade** with all ~1,300 campers in uniform 

● Horses, marching units, and **aviation flyover** 

**Supervision, discipline & support** 

**Cabin & dorm supervision** 

● Woodcraft: **9 staff per 60 campers** in each unit (1 counselor sleeps in-cabin) ● Upper Camp: counselors live directly in dorms/barracks with campers 

**Behavior & discipline** 

● Discipline framed as **learning and growth**, not punishment 

● Interventions first; separation only if a camper cannot re-engage ● Some campers placed on **disciplinary contracts** as structured second chances 

**Emotional Support Services (ESS)** 

● **Year-round licensed counselors & social workers** 

● Two health centers in summer (main campus + Woodcraft) 

● Required counseling for some campers based on disclosed needs ● ESS staff embedded in daily life; strong support for: 

○ homesickness

○ anxiety 

○ transitions 

● Camp may **decline placement** if needs exceed what Culver can responsibly support 

**Community & culture notes** 

● **Co-ed programs**, but cabins assigned by **sex assigned at birth** 

● No gender-neutral cabins; classes and activities are co-ed 

● **English is the official language** in public spaces 

● Campers from **40+ states and ~30 countries** 

**Parent access & visitation** 

● **Two permit weekends** during the summer: 

○ after week 2 

○ after week 4 

● Parents may sign campers out during the day and return them by evening 

**Transportation** 

● **Chicago O’Hare (ORD)** is the designated airport 

● Culver runs **12–15 charter buses** for arrivals and departures 

● Families simply book flights to/from O’Hare

**Positioning / who thrives here** 

**Best fit for:** 

● kids ready for **structure, routine, and accountability** 

● families seeking **deep character + leadership development** ● campers who benefit from **technology-free immersion** 

● students who may later reference Culver meaningfully in **college essays** 

**Important to flag for families:** 

● long **6-week commitment only** 

● strong structure and expectations 

● not a casual or drop-in camp experience 

● leadership growth may involve **discomfort before confidence**

title: Chantemerle Recap
text:
**Chantemerle (Switzerland) — Summer Camp + School-Year Study Abroad** 

**At a glance** 

● **Where:** Swiss boarding school campus with **lake views**, near **Montreux / Lake Geneva**, easy access to **Geneva** + nearby **ski resorts**. 

● **What it is:** An **educational summer camp**—**learning in the morning**, **activities in the afternoon**, with **optional sports academies** for kids who want a more intensive sport focus. 

● **Boarding size (school year):** Small and personal—**~60–65 boarders max**. 

**Summer Camp: program structure** 

**Morning learning options (students can mix depending on length):** 

● **Languages: French** (main), plus **English** and **German** (practical, project-oriented, not overly formal). 

● **Leadership / life skills track:** Workshops like **debates, cooking challenges, outdoor/survival**, tech projects, and team-building/time management (built for students who don’t want 4 hours/day of language class). 

● **Math + Physics revision:** More technical academic option, often middle-school oriented but with some older students; taught with teachers from **EPFL/Polytechnic Lausanne** background. 

**Afternoons + evenings:** 

● Classic Swiss “outdoor-first” activity mix: **mountains + lake**, plus some on-campus activities. 

● Signature highlight: a **2-day trip to Europa-Park (Germany)** (a “big deal” for many campers).

**Sports academies (optional add-on)** 

● Partnered with **local professional academies** (e.g., **basketball, football/soccer**), plus popular options like **tennis, horse riding, and golf**. 

● Some academies are **full-day** (and during that week students **may not** do the morning learning program, and **may miss Europa Park** if dates don’t align). 

**Ages, length, and dates** 

● **Typical summer age range: ~8/9 up to 16/17** (they mentioned “we take from 6,” but practically summer starts closer to **8**). 

● **Session length: 2 weeks minimum**, up to **5 weeks**. 

● **Season: End of June through early August**. 

**Accommodations & supervision** 

● **Rooms: 3–4 beds** in summer, **mostly ensuite** bathrooms (except one smaller-kids setup). 

● **Style:** Simple, comfortable (not “5-star” luxury). 

● **Supervision:** Traditional and structured—**night watch** + educators on-site; students are **supervised and in groups** (not a “go off on your own at night” type program). 

● **Day camp option:** Available; ~**10%** choose it (often families staying nearby in Montreux—e.g., during the **Montreux Jazz Festival**—and dropping off/picking up daily). 

**Student mix** 

● Summer is described as **fully international** and varies year to year: 

○ Commonly: **Spain, Italy, France**, other Europeans, plus **Asia, Africa, South America**, some **Japan**, and a **small US contingent** (often **~3–4 US students** in

a typical year). 

**Pricing (summer)** 

● Quoted example: **2 weeks ≈ $4,500**, **all-in except**: 

○ **Sports academies** (add-on) 

○ **Medical insurance** 

**School-year option (Study Abroad / Boarding School)** 

● Two main pathways: 

○ **Swiss pathway (in French) → Swiss Matura** (globally recognized; they highlighted that it can provide access to Swiss public universities). 

○ **English pathway:** smaller **British track** (they referenced **GCSE/A-Level**). 

● **Study abroad stays:** options like **1 month / 1 term / 3 months / 5 months / 1 year** (priced proportionally off the annual rate). 

● **Annual tuition + boarding: 55,500 CHF** (their stated base figure). 

● Best-fit note (from their discussion): US families more often consider this at **~ages 12–15** (pre–high school) or in specific “transition/gap” situations. 

**Who it fits best** 

● Students who want a **structured, supervised European boarding-style summer** with **language learning + outdoor Switzerland**. 

● Families who value **small scale + personalization** and a **more traditional oversight model**.

● Students who either: 

○ want **languages**, or 

○ want a **non-traditional morning option** (leadership/life skills), or ○ want **serious academic refresh** (math/physics), or 

○ want an **intensive sport week** via academy add-ons.

title: Camp Zeke Recap
text:
**Overview of Camp Zeke** 

• **Theme**: Celebrating healthy, active living through: 

o **Cooking** 

o **Fitness** 

o **Sports** 

o **Arts** 

• **Elective-Based Schedule**:
