o Campers design their day based on their interests. 

o Offers a balance between competitive sports, individual activities, and  creative arts. 

**Programs and Activities** 

***1. Culinary Arts*** 

• Facilities: 

o Three fully equipped teaching kitchens. 

• Activities: 

o Campers prepare dishes like sushi, cookies, bread, and granola bars from  scratch. 

o Annual cookbooks compile recipes for families to try at home. 

***2. Fitness and Individual Activities*** 

• **Martial Arts**: 

o Classes in fitness boxing, Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu, wrestling, and Krav Maga. o Dedicated martial arts gym. 

• **Strength Training**: 

o Fully equipped gym with personal trainers. 

o "Fitness Goals" program: Customized plans for campers to improve strength  and agility, tailored to personal interests (e.g., soccer training). 

• **Outdoor Adventure**: 

o Hiking, camping, outdoor cooking, and navigation. 

• **Gymnastics and Dance**: 

o Dance performances at the end of each three-week session.

o Floor routines, yoga, and dance classes. 

• **Circus Arts**: 

o Popular program including aerial silks, hoops, and straps. 

o Performances recorded and shared with families. 

***3. Outdoor Adventures*** 

• **Zip Line**: Runs across the main campus. 

• **Climbing and Bouldering**: Includes a new outdoor ninja ropes course. • **Horseback Riding**: 

o Lessons are 1 minute from camp (transport provided). 

o 2–3 lessons per week, pre-registration required. 

o Extra cost. 

***4. Water Activities*** 

• Location: Private 60-acre Hickory Lake. 

• Activities: Water skiing, tubing, canoeing, kayaking, sailing, and paddleboarding. • Heated Pool: 

o Swim instruction for campers below 6th grade is mandatory. 

o Optional swim team for older campers. 

o Additional swim lessons for campers failing the swim test. 

***5. Team Sports*** 

• Competitive programs in: 

o Soccer, volleyball, basketball, flag football, baseball, and tennis. • Participation in the Wayne County Camp Alliance for inter-camp competitions. • Recent Upgrades: 

o New tennis and basketball courts. 

o Enhanced coaching staff for sports programs. 

***6. Creative Arts*** 

• **Music**: 

o Music performances at the end of each session. 

• **Fine Arts**: 

o Painting, ceramics, crafts, tie-dye, and more.

• **Theater**: 

o Full musicals performed at the end of each session. 

o Professional theater director oversees the program. 

***7. Evening Programs*** 

• All-camp activities include: 

o Dance parties, movie nights, campfires with s’mores, Iron Chef  competitions, and July 4th fireworks. 

**Facilities** 

• **Location**: Wayne County, Pennsylvania. 

• **Bunks**: 

o Heated and air-conditioned. 

o Hotel-quality mattresses and private bathrooms. 

o 12–14 campers and 3–4 staff per bunk. 

o Organized into units with dedicated unit heads. 

• **Campus Map**: 

o Interactive map on the website, showing facilities and program areas. 

**Special Programs** 

***Taste of Zeke*** 

• **Duration**: One week. 

• **Target Audience**: Families hesitant to commit to three weeks for first-time  campers. 

• **Structure**: 

o Taste of Zeke campers have dedicated bunks based on age. 

o They rotate activities with corresponding age groups from the full program. • **Goal**: Provide a positive experience to encourage return for full sessions. • **Extensions**: 

o Campers can extend if there is space available, but this is not guaranteed.

**Jewish Life and Inclusivity** 

• **Kosher Kitchen**: 

o Fully supervised, ensuring all families feel welcome. 

• **Shabbat**: 

o Public areas maintain a “Shomer Shabbat” atmosphere. 

o Campers can follow personal practices in their bunks. 

o Activities are adjusted subtly on Saturdays to respect observant campers. • **Diversity**: 

o Camp welcomes families across the Jewish spectrum and non-Jewish  families. 

**Tuition and Financial Assistance** 

• **Transparency**: 

o Rates and Tuition Assistance prominently featured on the website. • **Scholarships**: 

o Robust scholarship program for families, including those with six-figure  incomes. 

o Designed to make camp accessible, reflecting the rising costs in the  industry. 

**Strategic Growth and Enrollment Goals** 

• **Core Sessions**: 

o 3-week and 6-week options, with increasing demand for full-season  enrollment. 

o Recent summers have reached capacity; the goal is to fill spots earlier in the  year. 

• **Expansion**: 

o Plans to increase capacity and improve facilities.

title: Camp Emerson
text:
**Key Updates and Improvements at Camp Emerson:** 

1\. **Program Development**: 

a. A strong focus on developing sports curriculum with structured progression  plans, moving away from unstructured play. 

b. Each sports session will focus on practicing specific skills, drills, and ending  with fun, ensuring campers feel they’ve learned something new. 

c. An emphasis on creating a safe environment for positive experiences,  including learning from failure. 

2\. **Activity Expansion**: 

a. Introduction of a culinary arts program in a newly constructed space with  professional-grade equipment. 

b. The return of inter-camp competitions with other Berkshire camps for variety  and challenge (not daily but periodic). 

3\. **Facilities Upgrades**: 

a. New **staff housing**: A structure called "The Quad" has replaced an outdated  facility with upgraded amenities. 

b. **New cabins on the girls' side**: These feature toilets and sinks, with similar  renovations planned across camp over time. 

c. **Zip line renovation**: A revamped zip line into Lake Emerson. 

d. **Dance studio renovation**: The studio has been gutted and rebuilt for an  enhanced arts experience. 

e. Plans to continue adding toilets and sinks in camper bunks while  maintaining existing high-quality bathhouses. 

4\. **Holistic Approach**: 

a. Activities span across sports, arts, and cabin life, ensuring a well-rounded  experience. 

b. Focus on shorter two-week sessions, with a fast-paced, engaging “sprint”  mentality to maximize camper enjoyment. 

c. Older campers (CITs) will take on leadership roles to inspire younger  campers. 

**Operational Details:** 

1\. **Elective Model**:

a. Campers pre-select activities and can adjust their schedules after arrival to  align with their interests or new friendships. 

b. Each elective session lasts 1 hour, with two sessions in the morning, two in  the afternoon, and a free time period before lunch. 

c. A structured “A/B schedule” allows for variety. Every 5th day is an “S Day”  (Special Day) with camp-wide activities and extra rest. 

2\. **Camper Demographics**: 

a. Last year, 260 total campers attended across sessions, with a mix of two week (65%) and four/six-week (35%) campers. Most campers opt for two week stays. 

b. Campers come from a broad geographic range, including international  locations (e.g., Paris, England, Ghana, Mexico, Canada). 

3\. **Activity Structure**: 

a. Activities span sports, arts, STEM (e.g., woodworking, rocketry, and science  experiments), culinary arts, water skiing, and wakeboarding. 

b. Water activities are held at a nearby lake, accessible via van transport. 4. **Special Trips for Older Campers**: 

a. CITs (age 16): A three-day trip to Vermont, including activities like hiking,  farm tours, and visiting the Ben & Jerry’s factory. 

b. Oldest campers (age 15): An overnight camping trip with activities like  mountain coasters and zip-lining. 

**Camper and Staff Support:** 

1\. **Dining and Dietary Accommodations**: 

a. Nut-free and gluten-free menus with vegetarian and vegan options. b. A dedicated dietary chef to handle allergen-friendly meals. 

c. A dietetic intern will assist with ensuring high-quality food service. d. Flexible meal options to ensure campers always find something to enjoy. 2. **Supervision and Safety**: 

a. Staff are stationed across camp to monitor transitions between activities. b. Attendance is taken at the start of every activity, with clear protocols if a  camper is late or absent. 

3\. **Staff Training**: 

a. A robust orientation includes mock scenarios for activities, meals, and  bedtime to ensure counselors are well-prepared.

b. Emphasis on building relationships among staff and ensuring they feel  confident and connected before campers arrive. 

4\. **Health and Medical Support**: 

a. Multiple nurses on-site and a doctor available for consultations. b. Urgent care is 10 minutes away, and a hospital is 18 minutes away. 5. **Inclusivity**: 

a. Case-by-case plans for transgender or gender-diverse campers, ensuring  personalized support and a safe environment. 

b. Private bathrooms and showers contribute to the camp’s inclusivity efforts. 

**Parent and Camp Expert Engagement:** 

1\. **Marketing and Communication**: 

a. Plans for a new promotional video and social media shorts to showcase  camp highlights. 

b. The co-directors are willing to travel to meet families, including visits to  South Florida, New York, and New Jersey. 

2\. **Camp Experience for Specific Groups**: 

a. The camp provides a welcoming environment for children with diverse  needs, such as dyslexia, by training staff to adapt communication and  teaching methods. 

3\. **Transportation Options**: 

a. Closest airports: Boston (preferred), Albany, and Hartford. 

b. Camp offers bus transportation from Newton, MA, and White Plains, NY, for  an additional fee.

title: Camp Arrow Wood
text:
**Camp Arrow Wood** 

**Location**: New York 

**Session Length**: Two 3-week sessions (6 weeks total) 

**Camp Size**: ~130 campers/session, with plans to grow to 250 

**Ideal For**: Motivated athletes who thrive in high-energy, sports-first environments **Gender**: Coed 

**Religious Affiliation**: None explicit; ~40–50% Jewish 

**Ages**: Middle school through rising 11th grade 

**Program Overview** 

Camp Arrow Wood is a **modern, sports-focused sleepaway camp** combining elite athletic training with traditional camp spirit. It offers both **intensive instruction** and **fun, structured gameplay**, with an emphasis on **competition with compassion**. Ideal for kids who are **active, competitive, and community-oriented**. 

**Core Sports** 

● Basketball, Soccer, Baseball, Softball, Football (Tackle & Flag), Tennis, Lacrosse, Golf ● Each led by a **college- or pro-level coach** 

● **Media & Sports Marketing elective** (e.g., highlight reels, sports broadcasting) 

**Electives & Activities** 

● Beach volleyball, field hockey, sports media, outdoor adventure 

● Full waterfront program: wakeboarding, trick skiing, sailing, kayaking ● Traditional camp events: color war, campfires, evening activities 

**Living & Structure** 

● 8 campers per bunk, 2 counselors 

● Bunked by age and gender

● Daily schedule includes: Intensive → Explore → Camper Choice → Game Time → Lake Time 

**Transportation** 

● Bus pickup from Westchester (Tarrytown/White Plains) 

● Airport pickups for national/international campers

title: Bryn Mawr Recap
text:
***Traditional Camp:*** 

**1. Structure and Offerings:** 

a. Operates as a full-season camp with a 5-week option for first-time campers,  most of whom extend their stay. 

b. Caters to campers entering **2nd grade (Mini Manor House)** up to **10th grade  (Bunk One)**. 

c. Features an **LIT program (Leaders-in-Training)** for 11th graders, including  leadership roles, program assistance, and a 10-day trip to Hawaii. 

**2. Programming and Schedules:** 

a. Younger campers (2nd–5th grade) follow structured schedules, while older  campers (6th grade and up) have more elective-based options. 

b. By 9th and 10th grades, schedules are nearly fully elective, allowing  customization and inclusion of group activities tailored to camper  

preferences. 

**3. Pullout Programs:** 

a. Specialized pullout instruction offered for activities like gymnastics, dance,  horseback riding, soccer, tennis, and basketball. 

b. These cater to campers with specific interests for more focused, small group instruction. 

**4. Retention and Recruitment:** 

a. Boasts a high **90% retention rate** year-over-year. 

b. Recruitment strategies include **tours** and a **Rookie Explorers Weekend**, a 2- night program to introduce potential campers to the camp experience. 

***Riding Camp:*** 

**1. Facilities and Capacity:** 

a. Operates at a dedicated **50-acre farm** with 30 horses and an **indoor riding  arena**, supplemented by 10 horses stabled at the main camp and 2  

additional arenas. 

b. Offers four 2-week sessions with a capacity of **88 campers per session**. **2. Program Highlights:** 

a. Focuses on high-quality instruction, advanced riding techniques (up to 3- foot jumps), and tailored lessons for riders of various skill levels.

b. Strong focus on safety and partnerships with secondary institutions like  Findlay University for high-caliber horses and training. 

**3. Camper Integration:** 

a. Riding campers dine with traditional campers but are housed and participate  in separate programs, maintaining autonomy. 

***Dance Camp:*** 

**1. Programming and Disciplines:** 

a. Offers instruction in various genres: **hip hop, ballet, jazz, tap, lyrical, and  musical theater**. 

b. Hip hop is the most popular genre among campers. 

**2. Session Goals and Performance:** 

a. The goal is to host **three sessions of 25 campers**. Last year, two sessions  were fully enrolled. 

b. Culminates in an end-of-session **performance for parents**, showcasing  camper progress. 

**3. Camper Dynamics:** 

a. Dance campers integrate with traditional campers in shared spaces like  dining but maintain program-specific activities and housing. 

***Gymnastics Program:*** 

**1. Facilities and Expertise:** 

a. Features a **40x40 spring vaulted floor**, tumble track, in-ground trampoline,  and foam pit. 

b. Hosts the **Wayne County Gymnastics Meet**, consistently winning titles for  over 40 years. 

c. Gymnastics director **Bill**, a veteran of 44 years, is lauded for his expertise  and rapport with campers. 

**2. Camper Fit:** 

a. Serves gymnasts up to **Level 5–7**. Advanced gymnasts aiming for elite-level  training typically transition to specialty programs like IGA camps.

**Camper and Staff Development** 

***Camper Support:*** 

• Emphasis on individualized attention, fostering resilience, communication skills,  and social responsibility. 

• Collaboration with parents and therapists for tailored support plans. • Dedicated programs like a **Friendship Coach** for relationship-building and one-on one social activities. 

***Staff Support:*** 

**1. Hiring and Training:** 

a. Staff are selected for qualities like warmth, empathy, adaptability, and  dedication to camper care. 

b. Comprehensive orientation and continuous training throughout the summer. **2. Amenities and Support:** 

a. Amenities like a **staff lounge, coffee bar, lockers**, and outdoor relaxation  spaces. 

b. Organized **staff trips** and social events to promote morale. 

c. A **staff coach** facilitates weekly meetings, check-ins, and workshops to  address challenges. 

**3. Behavioral Standards:** 

a. Strict policies to ensure safety and professionalism (e.g., no one-on-one  camper-staff interactions). 

b. Staff residing with campers are not permitted alcohol, while others may  enjoy limited drinks off duty under supervision. 

**Operational and Facility Updates** 

***Capital Improvements:*** 

• **Fieldhouse Revamp:** Converted storage into activity spaces (e.g., camper lounge,  cold cooking, sewing room). 

• **Dining Room:** $400,000 roof replacement for the 22,000 sq. ft. facility. • **New Additions:** Pickleball courts, new gym floor, and plans to air-condition the  gymnastics building.

• **Cold Cooking Program:** Added as a separate activity from hot cooking to meet  demand. 

***Camp Group Ownership:*** 

• Recently acquired by Camp Group, which provides back-end support like payroll,  insurance, and staffing resources. 

• Operational decisions remain with the Kagan family, ensuring continuity of Bryn  Mawr’s culture and standards. 

**Cultural and Strategic Emphasis** 

• **Single-Sex Environment:** Unique positioning as the only single-sex camp in Wayne  County. 

• **Community Integration:** Efforts to integrate specialty and traditional camp  communities while maintaining distinct programming. 

• **Staff-Camper Culture:** Strong focus on fostering a **sisterhood** and creating an  inclusive, supportive atmosphere. 

**Food and Dietary Accommodations** 

• **Dietary Needs:** Separate kitchen for gluten-free meals; accommodates dairy, soy,  and sesame allergies. 

• **Snack Program:** Introduced morning and evening snacks to address hunger outside  traditional meal times. 

• **Cooking Activities:** Cold cooking includes smoothies, acai bowls, and vegetable  sushi.

title: Brown Ledge Recap
text:
**Brown Ledge Camp — at a glance** 

**Type:** Traditionally all-girls overnight camp (inclusive of anyone who can feel safe and thrive in that program) 

**Location:** Mallets Bay on Lake Champlain (Colchester, VT), near Burlington **Big headline:** 100% freedom-of-choice programming + standout equestrian, theater, and waterfront 

**What makes Brown Ledge different** 

**“Freedom of choice” — truly 100%.** 

This isn’t “pick your schedule.” Campers decide how they spend activity time: they can be at an activity area, relaxing on a bench reading, showering, or hanging in their cabin. Staff keep an eye out for campers who are disengaging too much, but the core premise is independence and self-direction. 

**Two activity blocks per day.** 

● Morning: ~9:30–12:00 

● Afternoon: ~2:30–5:00 

Riding lessons (for campers in equestrian) are the main “fixed-time” daily commitment; theater rehearsals happen afternoons/evenings for campers who choose theater. 

**Counselors don’t sleep in camper cabins.** 

Most camper cabins are small (typically 4–5 campers, same age). Counselors are assigned to cabins and check in at night, but don’t live inside the cabin—another intentional independence-builder. 

**The setting + facilities vibe** 

**Rustic, classic, and intentionally simple.** 

Cabins have electricity, but **no running water**. Camp uses **central bathrooms** for campers and separate bathrooms for staff (a few staff cabins have private bathrooms, but those are limited).

**“Feels remote” while still close to town.** 

Camp is about **8 miles from Burlington** and near local services (including a police station), which helps with both parent peace-of-mind and staff quality of life. 

**Size:** Max ~180 campers; recent summers ~155; target ~160 per session. 

**Sessions + ages** 

**Ages:** 10–17 

**Sessions:** Two **4-week** sessions, or **full 8 weeks**. 

**Typical mix:** 

● About **50 full-summer** (includes Junior Counselors) 

● About **100 changeover** each session 

**No short “intro session.”** 

It’s 4 or 8 weeks. 

**Signature philosophy: the achievement system** Brown Ledge has an **achievement progression** designed to reward depth and mastery: ● **Basic:** attainable after a few visits 

● **Intermediate:** typically ~3 weeks of focused effort 

● **Vanguard:** highest level (often full-summer older campers / junior counselors), includes **mastery + teaching the activity**; not everyone earns it 

The camp values trying, learning, and even “productive failure,” supported by **activity specialist staff** (staff are trained/experienced in the areas they teach). 

**Camper care + support**

**Campers are expected to handle a lot of independence.** 

Brown Ledge can be a great fit for campers who want autonomy, but it’s **not ideal for campers who need constant supervision and prompting**. 

**Dedicated Camper Care team (created post-COVID).** 

There are **three Camper Care adults** (often educators / similar backgrounds) whose role is supporting campers—homesickness, conflict resolution, general wellbeing, and cabin inspections/cleanliness support. 

**Activities families ask about most** 

**Equestrian (included — no extra fee)** 

● No additional cost 

● About **60–70 campers per session** participate 

● Riding is the key scheduled element each day for riders 

**Theater (a major marquee)** 

● High-level productions: 

○ First two weeks: **three one-act plays** 

○ Third week of session: **full-length play or musical** 

● Campers can participate, but **acting roles require auditions** 

**Waterfront on Lake Champlain** 

● **Water skiing + wakeboarding** 

● **Sailing** (including an overnight sailing trip on the larger lake) 

● **Diving instruction** (learning real dives—swan dives, backflips, etc.) + springboard ● Canoeing, kayaking, paddleboarding

● Sundays: more “fun waterfront” (tubing, hang/swim) rather than instruction 

● Swim area is deep-water dock entry (not a beach), and the camp owns an island that helps protect the swim zone from boat traffic 

● They noted fewer algae issues in their swim area than other parts of the lake 

**Swim instruction:** Yes, offered (and they do in-camp swim meets / “Aqua Cade” end-of-summer show featuring skiing/sailing/boating + synchronized swimming). 

**Other popular areas** 

● **Riflery** (.22s, live ammo) + **archery** 

● **Tennis** (4 clay courts; rebuilding program with a new tennis director) 

● **Arts & crafts** (includes deeper instruction—example: ceramics taught by a professional potter alum; also sewing) 

**Food + allergies** 

**Allergies and dietary needs:** They said they can accommodate “almost all / essentially all” food allergies. 

**Kitchen:** In-house staffed/managed, giving flexibility in meal prep. 

**Religion / culture (what to tell families)** 

**Non-denominational.** 

● Meals include singing and a “grace”-style moment that’s framed more around the earth/nature than God 

● Sunday “Chapel” is not religious—campers/staff share reflections and personal experiences

**Transportation options** 

● Fly into **Burlington airport** (camp can pick up) 

● **Amtrak to Burlington** + drive/pickup 

● **Montreal pickups** available (common for international/Canada families) 

● Historically had a **NYC-area bus**, but it hasn’t run recently due to low utilization; could be revisited if demand returns 

● They noted broader challenges finding charter bus drivers lately 

**Who tends to thrive here** 

A strong fit for campers who: 

● Want **real independence** and freedom to self-direct 

● Enjoy going deep in an activity (especially equestrian, theater, or waterfront) ● Are comfortable in a **rustic** setting (central bathrooms, no running water in cabins) ● Can handle a physically “walky” campus (it’s on a ledge; mobility matters) 

Not the best fit for campers who: 

● Need constant structure, frequent check-ins, or high-supervision support 

● Have mobility limitations that make navigating camp difficult (they’ve even had ankle injuries that required going home because the terrain makes it hard) 

**Enrollment notes (helpful context for positioning)** ● Biggest feeder areas: **NYC metro**, then **Boston metro**

● Also draws from across the US + Canada (and some Europe: France/England/Spain) ● **Retention:** shared as ~67% last year 

● Second session is currently lower due to **school calendar timing** (camp ending around Aug 19 was mentioned) 

**Best point of contact** 

**Brooks Johnson (Camp Director)** currently handles **enrollment and family communication directly** (email + text are both fine).

title: Birchmont (Zoom - Nov. 4th)
text:
**Camp Birchmont — Recap (Will Pierce)** Prepared from the Zoom with Director/Owner Will Pierce. 

**Snapshot** 

Co-ed, family-owned, ACA-accredited sleepaway camp on Lake Wentworth in Wolfeboro, New  Hampshire. Target size ~275 campers per session (capacity ~300). Seven-week full season, with  4-week (first) and 3-week (second) options; a tightly managed two-week “Rookie” on-ramp for  first-timers (two per bunk; many elect to extend). Community is intentionally diverse by  geography and faith; culture emphasizes first-principles character development and confidence. 

**Leadership & Sales Approach** 

• **Director/Owner:** Will Pierce (taking primary lead on family sales/relations), with  Director Greg Howes supporting overflow and program work. 

• **Access:** Will intends to be “in the room or on the Zoom” with prospective families; open  to direct coordination with referral partners. 

• **Why now:** Birchmont has long been a “hidden gem”; the team wants to keep the  intimacy while removing the “too hidden” part. 

**Mission & Culture** 

• **Three C’s:** *Community. Confidence. Camp.* 

• **Welcome ethos:** “There are no strangers within—only friends waiting to be met.” This  greets every camper on arrival and sets the tone for cross-regional bunk communities. • **Confidence challenge:** First-night campfire prompt—“What would you attempt to do if  you knew you could not fail?” Campers state a summer goal; staff coach toward  achievement. Weekly “quiet hour” gatherings reinforce reflection and growth. 

**Location & Facilities** 

• **Wolfeboro, NH (America’s oldest summer resort):** 7 miles from Maine; ~50 miles to  Portland (PWM); ~90 miles to Boston (BOS); full-service hospital 7 miles from camp. • **Lake Wentworth waterfront:** ~½-mile private shoreline on a clean, warm (≈78–82°F)  natural-sand lake with restricted public access—quiet and safe for waterfront instruction. • **Campus scale:** 300+ acres including **Mount Delight** on property (hiking, orienteering,  lean-tos, blackberry picking), 100-tree orchard, organic garden. 

• **Program differentiators (existing):** Equestrian center (6 horses; trails + ring), 12 lit  tennis courts, 250-yard driving range, large indoor lodge for performing arts/community. • **2026 capital projects (contracts in progress):** 

o **Large indoor sports complex** with full **gymnastics** center (addressing past  demand; designed for rainy-day coverage).

o **Outdoor covered dining pavilion** with pizza ovens and grills (supports evolving,  more fresh/local food service). 

o **Food vision (multi-year):** Grow farm-to-camp sourcing partnerships; “grilled  proteins + vegetables” emphasis while keeping mainstream options. 

o **Campfire amphitheater (orchard overlook):** Planned; **may slip to 2027** pending forestry/permitting cadence. 

**Program & Daily Life** 

• **Structured choice (not a free-elective model):** At every period (except required  morning swim), campers choose among several options; deeper electives (“Senior  Select”) begin at age ~13 (elective mornings + structured-choice afternoons). • **Advanced tracks available:** 

o **Riding:** 0–5 sessions/week by sign-up. 

o **Tennis:** HPI (High Performance Instruction) up to ~1 extra hour/day with a  USPTA pro. 

o **Golf:** On-site range plus weekly play on local courses. 

o **Aquatics:** Robust progression; lifeguard certification available (25 grads last  season). Teens who pass the swim test may opt out of instruction—but most still  participate. 

• **Evening “Clubs” (2x/week):** 20 staff-led micro-electives (e.g., rocketry, fishing,  gardening, ukulele, dodgeball), chosen on the night—varies with staff passions. • **Mobility & resilience:** Birchmont **owns its bus company**, enabling ready intercamp  playdates, rainy-day trips (movies/bowling), and defined emergency relocation  (Kingswood Regional HS shelter, ~5 miles). 

**Sessions, Admissions & Community** 

• **Season structure:** 7-week full; 4-week (first) and 3-week (second) sessions. • **Rookie Camp (2 weeks, first-timers only):** Two per bunk to protect dynamics; rookies  arrive with second-session campers and can **extend to stay the final week**. o **Case-by-case** early-session two-week starts are possible when calendars demand,  but departures mid-bonding are avoided when feasible. 

• **Camper profile:** Families seeking independence and cross-regional friendships more  than “arrive with a crowd.” Birchmont is **non-denominational** and intentionally mixed  by faith and background. 

• **Scale:** ~275 campers/session, aiming toward a warm, know-every-child environment; no  plans to “go big.” 

**Transportation** 

• **Included buses:** Long Island, NYC, and Darien, CT. 

• **Air:** Staffed pickups at BOS and PWM; **chartered Florida flight** with chaperone.

**How to Position Birchmont to Families** 

• **Distinctive place + program:** A “New Hampshire classic” on a private, warm lake with  a mountain on property—paired with a structured-choice program that ensures breadth  early and autonomy as kids age. 

• **Confidence with substance:** Goal setting, reflection rituals, and visible growth arcs— beyond slogans. 

• **Right size, right mix:** ~275 campers/session fosters personal attention; cabins blend  regions/faiths; kids make *new* friends. 

• **Waterfront credibility:** Clean, warm lake; daily instruction; strong aquatics outcomes  (including lifeguard cert). 

• **2026 upgrades:** Indoor complex with gymnastics and a covered outdoor dining venue  modernize rainy-day and food experiences without losing camp feel. 

**Notes We’re Keeping Handy with Families** 

• **Structure:** Not a “do anything anytime” elective camp; it’s *guided choice* with  increasing autonomy in the teens. 

• **Rookie nuance:** Two per bunk; designed to encourage extension rather than “two-weeks forever.” 

• **Travel:** Buses from LI/NYC/Darien; BOS/PWM airport service; chartered Florida  option. 

• **Diversity:** Co-ed, non-denominational, geographically broad; kids typically arrive with  few (or no) hometown friends—by design. 

**Open Items to Track (Internal)** 

• **CapEx timeline:** Indoor complex + gymnastics + covered dining **promised for Summer  2026**; amphitheater likely **2027** if permits slow. Confirm milestones in spring  photos/updates. 

• **Rookie intake guardrails:** Keep clarity on the “two per bunk” limit and extension  strategy; document any case-by-case early-session exceptions. 

• **Structured-choice messaging:** Ensure families expecting free-elective models  understand how Birchmont’s approach works (and why it benefits younger campers). • **Florida flight:** Verify annual viability (carrier/chaperone logistics) as demand fluctuates.
